proposals.title: Negotiating Negative Status: A Mixed-Methods Study of Traditional Harm Reduction and HIV Risk Management Among Young Women Who Use Drugs
proposals.motivation: Despite the biomedical dominance of PrEP in HIV prevention, significant populations, including young women who use drugs, may not access or trust clinical interventions, relying instead on traditional peer-based strategies. Understanding these culturally embedded practices is critical for effective public health, as the failure to engage with them may exacerbate risk and widen health inequities. This is especially pertinent for women navigating the dual stigmas of drug use and sexual health, where identity and social meaning profoundly influence health behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: For young women who use drugs, the perceived empowerment and effectiveness of traditional, non-clinical harm reduction methods for maintaining an HIV-negative status are associated with a stronger, more stable HIV-negative identity, and seroconversion disrupts these practices more profoundly than it does for those reliant on biomedical methods like PrEP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study with embedded qualitative interviews, recruiting 200 young women (aged 18-30) who use drugs and are HIV-negative at baseline, oversampling for those reporting non-use of PrEP. Quantitative surveys at 0, 6, and 12 months will measure drug use patterns, sexual behaviors, harm reduction strategies (using a novel scale categorizing 'traditional' vs. 'biomedical' methods), and psychosocial factors. We will use multivariate logistic regression to identify predictors of seroconversion and Cox proportional hazards models to analyze time-to-event. Concurrently, in-depth interviews with a purposive subsample (n=40) will explore the social meaning of 'HIV-negative' status and the logic of traditional practices.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate novel empirical data on the prevalence and perceived efficacy of traditional harm reduction among a high-risk, understudied population. It will challenge the assumed superiority of biomedical interventions by demonstrating the functional and identity-sustaining roles of peer-based methods. The findings will provide a framework for developing more culturally resonant, hybrid prevention programs that integrate, rather than dismiss, existing community knowledge.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely centers the lived experience and identity stability of young women who use drugs, moving beyond a deficit model to investigate the functional logic of their traditional practices. It directly compares the psychosocial and behavioral outcomes of traditional versus biomedical risk management strategies in a longitudinal design, a focus absent from pre-2026 chemsex and PrEP research centered primarily on men who have sex with men.

proposals.title: Testing a Risk-Salience Intervention to Increase Condom Use During Chemsex Among MSM in England: A Randomised Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is associated with high-risk sexual behaviours and increased incidence of STIs, including HIV. Despite widespread availability of condoms and PrEP, condom use remains inconsistent in these contexts, partly due to complex social and symbolic meanings attached to condoms. There is a need for targeted, theory-driven behavioural interventions that reframe condoms as a specific risk-mitigation tool within high-risk scenarios, rather than a broad symbol of disease or promiscuity.
proposals.hypothesis: An SMS-based behavioural intervention that explicitly pairs vivid, scenario-specific risk information with actionable condom-use strategies will increase consistent condom use during chemsex among MSM, compared to standard sexual health information.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, parallel-group randomised controlled trial (RCT) with 500 MSM in England who report recent chemsex. The intervention arm will receive a 12-week 'safetxt'-style SMS program, delivering messages grounded in behavioural economics. Messages will be designed to: 1) elicit negative affect by vividly describing STI risks specific to chemsex (e.g., prolonged, disinhibited sex), 2) immediately pair this with a clear, normative action (condom use as the 'intelligent tool' for this scenario), and 3) provide implementation intentions (e.g., 'When I prepare for a session, I will put condoms next to my phone'). The control arm will receive standard sexual health SMS tips. The primary outcome will be self-reported consistent condom use during chemsex at 3-month follow-up, analysed using logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first RCT evidence on the efficacy of a risk-salience messaging intervention to increase condom use in the high-priority chemsex context. It will contribute to the behavioural science of sexual health by testing a novel application of loss-aversion and implementation intention principles. Findings will directly inform scalable, low-cost public health messaging for hard-to-reach populations engaged in high-risk behaviours.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic condom promotion, this intervention deliberately leverages the condom's disease-prevention association, reframing it from a broad stigma to a context-specific facilitator of intelligent risk management. It adapts the 'graphic warning' analogy from tobacco control to a sexual health context where perceived risk is high but behavioural translation is low.

proposals.title: The 'Risk-Triggered' Condom: A Randomised Controlled Trial of a Behavioural Intervention Leveraging Disease Association to Increase Condom Use During Chemsex Among MSM on PrEP
proposals.motivation: Among men who have sex with men (MSM) on PrEP, condom use has declined, particularly during chemsex, heightening STI risk. Pre-2026, behavioural interventions often promote consistent condom use, but this can be ineffective in high-arousal, disinhibited contexts. This study addresses the need for novel, context-sensitive strategies that work with, rather than against, the psychological landscape of risk perception in these situations.
proposals.hypothesis: That a just-in-time adaptive intervention (JITAI) delivered via smartphone, which frames condom use as a specific tool for high-risk perceived situations (like chemsex) rather than universal practice, will increase situational condom use compared to standard safer sex messaging among MSM on PrEP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month RCT with 400 MSM on PrEP who report recent chemsex. The intervention arm receives a JITAI via a study app. Using ecological momentary assessment (EMA), participants report planned or imminent sexual situations. A rules-based algorithm triggers tailored messages that leverage the 'disease association' of condoms, framing them as the 'risk-management tool' for that specific high-risk context. The control arm receives standardised fortnightly safer sex information. The primary outcome is the proportion of condom-protected chemsex acts, measured via weekly behavioural surveys.
proposals.contribution: This will provide empirical evidence on whether reframing condom use as a targeted, risk-responsive behaviour (rather than a universal norm) is an effective strategy in the PrEP era. It will contribute a novel, technology-delivered behavioural intervention component specifically designed for chemsex contexts, a significant gap in current sexual health promotion.
proposals.novelty: It innovates by strategically leveraging the negative 'disease association' of condoms as a facilitator for use in specific high-risk situations, a reversal of traditional approaches that seek to dissociate condoms from disease. It also applies a JITAI framework, a method used in other health fields, to the precise moment of sexual risk decision-making among MSM.

proposals.title: Cognitive interviewing to refine a behavioural intervention for PrEP adherence and sexual health among women engaging in chemsex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, chemsex (using drugs before/during sex) is a growing public health concern associated with sexual risk behaviours and STIs, primarily studied in MSM. Women, particularly young women, are an under-researched population in this context. Effective behavioural interventions, like text-message support (safetxt), require clear, comprehensible health messaging, but understanding of terms related to contraception, PrEP, and drug-related sexual risk among this group is poorly documented, potentially undermining intervention efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: Cognitive interviewing with young women who engage in chemsex will identify significant misunderstandings and interpretation errors in key terms and concepts used in sexual health and PrEP adherence interventions, and these findings can be used to refine intervention materials to improve clarity and effectiveness.
proposals.method: We will adapt cognitive interviewing techniques from survey methodology. Following a prospective trial design (AURAH2/safetxt), we will recruit 25-30 women aged 18-30 who report chemsex, purposively sampled for diversity. In one-on-one interviews, participants will 'think aloud' while reviewing draft intervention content (text messages, information sheets) on PrEP, contraception, and risk. Specific probes will target terms like 'serodiscordant,' 'U=U,' 'drug interaction,' and 'condomless sex.' Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis to categorize comprehension problems (e.g., literal misinterpretation, inferred meaning) and inform iterative material revision.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a validated set of core communication materials for sexual health interventions targeting women in chemsex contexts. It will contribute empirical data on health literacy gaps in this population, directly informing the development of more precise and effective behavioural trials. The work bridges Dr. Hart's expertise in chemsex, PrEP, and trial methodology with a rigorous qualitative method to address structural communication barriers.
proposals.novelty: While cognitive interviewing is established in survey design, its application to pre-test and refine digital behavioural interventions for sexual health in the specific, high-risk context of chemsex among women is novel. It moves beyond identifying associated behaviours (as in AURAH2) to actively improving the intervention tools themselves based on user comprehension.

proposals.title: A mixed-methods investigation of the pathway from online drug-related discourse to transactional sex among men who have sex with men in London: implications for PrEP adherence and STI risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research identifies chemsex as a significant risk environment for STIs among MSM, with online platforms facilitating drug procurement and sexual networking. However, the specific behavioral pathway linking online drug talk to transactional sex (exchange of sex for drugs/money) remains poorly understood, obscuring a critical nexus of drug-related harm, PrEP non-adherence, and structural vulnerability. This gap limits the development of targeted interventions for this high-risk subset.
proposals.hypothesis: Among MSM engaged in chemsex, specific patterns of online drug-related discourse (e.g., coded language, direct solicitation) are prospectively associated with subsequent engagement in transactional sex, and this pathway mediates the relationship between online platform use and composite adverse outcomes of STI incidence and PrEP use discontinuity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study (n=300) of MSM in London reporting chemsex, recruited via sexual health clinics and online. The primary exposure will be quantified via automated text analysis of participants' anonymized, consent-provided geosocial and chat app data from the prior 6 months, using a pre-2026 NLP pipeline (BERT-based classifiers fine-tuned on known drug and sexual intent lexicons) to flag drug-talk and transactional intent. Outcomes (transactional sex, STI diagnosis, PrEP adherence via pharmacy records) will be assessed at 6 and 12 months via clinical data linkage and computer-assisted self-interview. Pathway analysis will use structural equation modelling to test mediation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical model of a high-specificity risk pathway from digital communication to a severe chemsex-related behavior. It will generate novel data on the intersection of transactional sex and PrEP use in this context, informing the adaptation of behavioral interventions (like safetxt) and clinical consultations to address this specific sequence of risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates chemsex with STIs, this study uniquely isolates and operationalizes a pre-behavioral digital signal to prospectively study a stigmatized and hard-to-reach behavior (transactional sex), moving beyond broad correlations to a testable mechanistic pathway.

proposals.title: The influence of sexual network characteristics and partner-level factors on chemsex trajectories and associated sexual health risks among MSM in England: a prospective cohort study
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, associated with increased risk of HIV/STI transmission and other harms. Pre-2026 research, including Hart's work, has largely focused on individual-level predictors and interventions, treating risk as an individual attribute. This overlooks the critical role of social and sexual networks in shaping drug use behaviours and risk environments, creating a gap in understanding how risk is socially produced.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the stability, drug-use norms, and PrEP/HIV status distribution within an individual's sexual network, as well as partner-level behaviours during encounters, will significantly modify the relationship between an individual's chemsex use and subsequent sexual health risks, such as STI incidence and HIV seroconversion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month prospective cohort study of 500 HIV-negative or HIV-positive MSM in England who report chemsex. Using a modified egocentric network design, participants will provide baseline and 12-month data on their sexual networks (size, density, alters' chemsex/PrEP use). Partner-level factors (e.g., condom negotiation, drug use synchronicity) will be captured via brief event-level surveys following reported chemsex encounters. Primary outcomes are lab-confirmed STI incidence and HIV seroconversion. We will use multilevel survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models with random effects for participant) and longitudinal latent class analysis to model how network/partner variables interact with individual chemsex frequency to predict risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond individual-centric risk models by quantifying the contribution of network and partner dynamics to chemsex-related harm. It will provide empirical evidence to inform the development of novel, network-aware interventions, such as partner-delivered prevention messaging or dyadic counselling, which could complement existing individual-level behavioural approaches like the safetxt trial.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified individual predictors of chemsex, this study innovatively integrates egocentric network analysis with event-level partner data within a prospective cohort to directly test how risk is co-constructed within partnerships and networks, challenging the paradigm of risk as a purely individual property.

proposals.title: A cost-of-illness analysis of sexually transmitted infections and related complications attributable to chemsex among HIV-positive men who have sex with men in England
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among MSM is associated with increased sexual risk behaviours and higher incidence of STIs, including antibiotic-resistant infections. For HIV-positive MSM, this poses a significant clinical and public health challenge, but the direct economic burden on healthcare systems remains unquantified. An economic analysis is needed to inform resource allocation and the cost-effectiveness of targeted behavioural or harm-reduction interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that chemsex participation among HIV-positive MSM is associated with significantly higher annual healthcare costs for the treatment of STIs and related complications (e.g., psychiatric, injury) compared to non-participating HIV-positive MSM, and that these costs are driven by specific, high-cost clinical events.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked clinical and administrative data from participating sexual health and HIV clinics in England. Individuals will be identified from the AURAH2 study cohort. The primary outcome is total healthcare cost (NHS perspective) for STI/chemsex-complication treatment over a 2-year follow-up. Costs will be attached using national reference costs (e.g., NHS Reference Costs, PSSRU). We will use generalised linear models (GLM) with a gamma distribution and log link to estimate incremental costs associated with chemsex, adjusting for confounders (age, PrEP use, HIV viral suppression). A probabilistic sensitivity analysis will be conducted.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical estimate of the healthcare costs attributable to chemsex among a key population. It will identify the clinical drivers (e.g., LGV, hepatitis C, mental health crises) of highest economic impact. The findings will supply crucial data for future economic evaluations of chemsex-specific interventions, supporting evidence-based policy and service planning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hart et al. has established behavioural predictors of chemsex, no study has applied health economic methods to quantify its direct financial burden on the NHS. This moves beyond association to quantify resource impact, integrating clinical epidemiology with health economics in a high-priority area.

proposals.title: Event-Level Pharmacological and Situational Determinants of Condomless Anal Sex Among MSM Engaged in Chemsex: A Mixed-Methods Diary Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, establishes strong population-level associations between chemsex (sexualized drug use) and increased HIV/STI risk among men who have sex with men (MSM). However, these studies cannot determine if unprotected sex is causally triggered by specific drugs, doses, sequences, or immediate social contexts within a chemsex event. This micro-level causal mechanism is critical for designing effective, event-specific harm-reduction interventions beyond PrEP promotion.
proposals.hypothesis: Within chemsex events among MSM, the occurrence of condomless anal sex (CAS) is predicted by specific, time-linked factors: 1) the use and timing of particular stimulants (e.g., mephedrone, crystal methamphetamine), 2) the subjective intensity of drug effects, and 3) situational factors like partner type and setting, over and above general participant-level risk profiles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective 28-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) diary study with 150 MSM reporting recent chemsex, recruited via sexual health clinics and apps. Participants will complete brief smartphone surveys morning and evening, and event-contingent surveys immediately after each sexual event, detailing drugs used (type, timing, dose), subjective effects, partner characteristics, and sexual behaviors. Analysis will use multilevel logistic regression modeling, nesting events within participants, to identify immediate pharmacological and situational predictors of CAS. A qualitative component with a sub-sample will explore decision-making narratives.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond identifying 'chemsex' as a monolithic risk factor to pinpoint the specific, modifiable within-event mechanisms that elevate HIV/STI risk. It will provide empirical evidence to tailor real-time behavioural interventions (e.g., just-in-time adaptive interventions via text) and inform clinical counselling by identifying the highest-risk drug combinations and contexts.
proposals.novelty: While previous work, including the AURAH2 study, analyzes starting/stopping chemsex over time, this proposal applies a micro-temporal, event-level lens. It integrates precise pharmacological timing with situational data, a method not yet widely applied in chemsex research pre-2026, to model causal pathways at the granularity of a single sexual encounter.

proposals.title: Co-designing and evaluating a community-led digital intervention for drug-related sexual harm (DRSH) and STI screening among MSM engaging in chemsex: a mixed-methods CBPR study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence indicates a syndemic of chemsex, HIV (including among PrEP users), and bacterial STIs among MSM, with drug-related sexual harm (DRSH) a significant concern. Existing sexual health services are often not designed to address the complex interplay of substance use and sexual risk in this population, leading to gaps in screening and acceptable interventions. Community-based participatory research (CBPR) is critical to develop relevant, non-stigmatizing strategies that address structural barriers to care.
proposals.hypothesis: A digital behavioural intervention for DRSH and STI screening, co-designed with MSM community organizations and delivered via SMS/MMS, will be more acceptable, feasible, and effective at increasing regular STI screening uptake and reducing self-reported DRSH incidents compared to standard signposting information among MSM who report recent chemsex.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods CBPR design. Phase 1: Form a community advisory board (CAB) with key MSM community organization partners to co-design intervention content and delivery via a series of workshops, informed by the safetxt trial framework and behavioural change techniques. Phase 2: Conduct a pilot randomised controlled trial (RCT). We will recruit 200 MSM reporting chemsex in the past 6 months via community channels and clinics. Participants will be randomised 1:1 to receive the 3-month co-designed SMS/MMS intervention or a control arm of standard sexual health signposting. Primary outcomes will be acceptability (validated scale) and feasibility (recruitment/retention rates). Secondary outcomes include STI screening uptake (verified via clinic record linkage where possible) and self-reported DRSH (using adapted measures from AURAH2).
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, community-owned intervention protocol for a high-risk, hard-to-reach population, directly addressing the 'missing men' gap in services. It will provide preliminary efficacy data on a scalable digital tool to integrate STI screening and harm reduction for chemsex, contributing to the literature on CBPR in sexual health and complex behavioural interventions beyond HIV prevention alone.
proposals.novelty: While CBPR is used in HIV, its application to co-design interventions specifically targeting the chemsex-STI syndemic and DRSH is novel. Furthermore, adapting the successful safetxt RCT model—focused on STI reinfection—to a primary prevention and harm reduction context for a defined chemsex-engaged population represents a significant methodological innovation.

proposals.title: Assessing the role of service-level factors in reinforcing masculine norms and deterring engagement with sexual health and chemsex support among men who have sex with men in England: a mixed-methods study
proposals.motivation: Despite high rates of STIs and HIV acquisition risk among men who have sex with men (MSM), engagement with sexual health services remains suboptimal, particularly for sensitive issues like chemsex. Pre-2026 research, including Hart's work on predictors of chemsex and 'missing men' in TB care, highlights structural and behavioural barriers but often focuses on individual-level factors. A critical gap exists in understanding how the design, delivery, and environment of health services themselves may inadvertently uphold masculine norms (e.g., stoicism, avoidance of help-seeking) that actively deter men, creating an 'inverse' problem where services meant to attract instead repel.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that specific, modifiable features of sexual health and chemsex support services (e.g., clinic environments, communication materials, consultation scripts) are perceived by MSM as reinforcing traditional masculine norms, and that these perceptions are directly associated with lower intentions to engage with and adhere to services, including PrEP programmes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase, explanatory sequential mixed-methods study. Phase 1: A cross-sectional survey with 500 MSM in England, recruited via community apps and clinics, using validated scales (e.g., Male Role Norms Inventory) and novel items to quantify perceptions of service masculinity and deterrents. We will use multivariate logistic regression to model associations between these perceptions and self-reported service avoidance. Phase 2: In-depth interviews (n=30-40) with a purposive sample of survey participants, analysed via reflexive thematic analysis to explicate the mechanisms through which services reinforce norms. The integration will use a joint display to map qualitative themes onto quantitative results.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond cataloguing patient-level barriers to identify actionable, service-level determinants of disengagement. It will provide a novel framework for auditing and redesigning sexual health services to be more gender-transformative, directly informing interventions to improve uptake of testing, PrEP, and chemsex support. The findings will contribute to the structural interventions literature highlighted in Hart's prior work on tuberculosis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines how masculinity affects health behaviours, this study uniquely inverts the lens to investigate how health services actively reproduce and are perceived to reinforce those norms. It applies structural analysis typically used in HIV/TB care for 'missing men' to the under-studied area of sexual health service design for MSM in high-income settings.

proposals.title: Modelling the impact of variable primary HIV infection infectiousness on transmission risk among MSM engaging in chemsex: a data-driven simulation study
proposals.motivation: Mathematical models of HIV transmission often assume a constant, high infectiousness during primary HIV infection (PHI), simplifying a complex biological reality. This assumption may misestimate transmission risk, particularly in high-risk networks like men who have sex with men (MSM) engaging in chemsex, where behavioural patterns intersect with acute infection. A more nuanced understanding of PHI infectiousness is critical for accurately evaluating the population-level impact of prevention tools like PrEP and for targeting behavioural interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a non-linear, time-varying infectiousness profile during PHI, informed by empirical viral load data, will significantly alter model estimates of HIV transmission risk and prevention effectiveness in MSM chemsex networks compared to models using a constant infectiousness assumption.
proposals.method: We will develop an individual-based network simulation using pre-2026 data on MSM sexual behaviour and chemsex from studies like AURAH2. The core innovation is the implementation of a piecewise or spline-based function for transmission probability per act, parameterized using meta-analyses of PHI viral load trajectories. This function will define infectiousness as low initially, peaking sharply, and then declining. We will compare transmission outcomes (incidence, cluster size) from this variable model against a standard constant-infectiousness model within the same simulated behavioural network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more biologically plausible modelling framework for HIV transmission dynamics in high-risk populations. It will generate refined estimates of the preventable fraction of transmissions attributable to PHI under different prevention scenarios, directly informing the prioritization and messaging of public health interventions targeting MSM, especially those using PrEP or engaging in chemsex.
proposals.novelty: While PHI is recognized as highly infectious, existing behavioural and epidemiological models rarely move beyond a simple 'high constant risk' window. This study integrates detailed virological kinetics into a behavioural simulation focused on a key risk group (chemsex-engaged MSM), creating a novel synthesis of biological and behavioural data to challenge a foundational modelling assumption.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in trends of condomless anal sex among men who have sex with men in England: a latent class analysis of longitudinal behavioural data
proposals.motivation: The widespread availability of PrEP and U=U messaging has been associated with population-level increases in condomless anal sex (CAS) among men who have sex with men (MSM). This aggregate trend may mask critical heterogeneity, potentially concentrating risk in specific, under-identified subgroups or partnership contexts. Understanding this heterogeneity is essential for designing targeted, effective sexual health interventions that move beyond one-size-fits-all risk reduction models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the observed population-level rise in CAS is not uniform but is concentrated within specific, identifiable subgroups of MSM, defined by intersecting behavioural (e.g., chemsex, PrEP use), demographic, and partnership (e.g., serodiscordant, casual vs. main) characteristics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal behavioural data from the AURAH2 prospective study. Using latent class growth analysis (LCGA), we will identify distinct trajectories of CAS over time. We will then use multinomial logistic regression to model class membership, with predictors including chemsex engagement, PrEP use, HIV status, partnership type, and demographic factors. This will quantify how behavioural trends cluster and identify the key drivers of concentrated CAS increases.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond aggregate descriptions to provide a nuanced, person-centred map of CAS trends. It will identify which MSM subpopulations are experiencing the most significant behavioural shifts, informing the targeting of resources and the development of tailored behavioural interventions. The findings will directly address the 'missing men' challenge in sexual health by clarifying which groups require specific engagement strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified predictors of CAS, this analysis innovatively applies longitudinal mixture modelling to test the non-uniformity of population trends, directly inverting the common assumption of a homogeneous behavioural shift. It integrates Dr. Hart's expertise in chemsex, PrEP, and trial-based behavioural analysis to dissect this complex trend.

proposals.title: Dynamic Partner Change Rates in MSM: A Longitudinal Analysis of Chemsex, PrEP Use, and STI Diagnosis as Drivers of Behavioural Flux
proposals.motivation: Mathematical models of STI transmission and behavioural interventions for MSM often rely on static partner change rates (PCRs), a significant simplification. Real-world PCRs are dynamic, influenced by life events, substance use (e.g., chemsex), and health status (e.g., an STI diagnosis). Using pre-2026 data, this study addresses a key methodological gap to improve the realism of risk prediction and intervention targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: Among MSM, partner change rates are not static but vary significantly over time, with identifiable periods of acceleration and deceleration directly associated with chemsex episodes, initiation or cessation of PrEP, and recent STI diagnoses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal behavioural data (e.g., from the AURAH2 study). Using multi-level growth curve models and latent class trajectory analysis, we will model PCR as a time-varying outcome. We will specify time-lagged covariates for chemsex engagement (binary, past 3 months), PrEP use status, and recent STI diagnosis (past 6 months) to test for temporal associations with changes in PCR. Sensitivity analyses will use joint modelling to account for participant dropout.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, quantitative estimates of how key behavioural and clinical factors modulate partnership dynamics in MSM over time. The findings will directly inform more accurate parameters for transmission dynamic models and highlight critical 'windows of risk' for targeted behavioural interventions, such as safetxt adaptations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies predictors of starting/stopping chemsex, this analysis uniquely models the partner change rate itself as a dynamic process, formally testing infection-status (STI diagnosis) as a behavioural feedback mechanism, moving beyond static cross-sectional risk factors.

proposals.title: A behavioural intervention trial to reduce HIV transmission risk among undiagnosed men who have sex with men (MSM) engaging in chemsex: the CHASM study
proposals.motivation: Undiagnosed men who have sex with men (MSM) are estimated to account for the majority of new HIV transmissions. This group is disproportionately represented in high-risk contexts like chemsex, where drug use is associated with condomless sex and PrEP discontinuation. Pre-2026, few prevention efforts are designed specifically to reach and engage this hidden, high-incidence population before diagnosis.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted, low-threshold behavioural intervention delivered via a trusted digital platform can effectively reduce HIV transmission risk behaviours and increase HIV/STI testing frequency among undiagnosed MSM who engage in chemsex.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic randomised controlled trial. Using respondent-driven sampling and geosocial networking apps, we will recruit MSM reporting recent chemsex and no HIV diagnosis. Participants will be randomised to intervention or standard information. The intervention is a theory-based (e.g., IMB model) package delivered via encrypted messaging, featuring: 1) personalised feedback on risk, 2) interactive modules on harm reduction for chemsex, 3) real-time access to testing location services, and 4) motivational messaging. Primary outcomes, measured at 6 and 12 months, will be condomless anal sex with serodiscordant/unknown status partners and self-reported HIV/STI testing frequency, analysed using intention-to-treat mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first RCT evidence on an intervention specifically designed for undiagnosed, chemsex-engaged MSM. It will provide a scalable model for reaching hidden populations and directly inform public health strategies aiming to interrupt transmission at its source. Findings will also elucidate the behavioural pathways linking chemsex, testing avoidance, and HIV risk.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Hart's prior work identified predictors of chemsex, this trial actively intervenes in that context. It innovates by targeting the pre-diagnosis phase—a critical gap—and leverages digital delivery to meet participants where they are, moving beyond observational studies to experimental causal evidence.

proposals.title: A behavioural intervention to reduce HIV transmission during primary infection among men who have sex with men engaging in chemsex: a randomised controlled trial
proposals.motivation: Primary HIV infection (PHI) is a period of exceptionally high infectiousness, contributing disproportionately to onward transmission. Men who have sex with men (MSM) engaging in chemsex are at elevated risk for HIV acquisition and transmission, yet interventions specifically targeting this group during the critical PHI window are lacking. Reducing transmission at this early stage is crucial for HIV epidemic control, especially in high-risk networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted, theory-based behavioural intervention delivered via a hybrid model (initial in-person session + digital follow-up) to MSM diagnosed with PHI and reporting chemsex will be more effective than standard of care in reducing self-reported condomless anal sex acts with serodiscordant/unknown status partners and increasing rapid ART initiation over a 3-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm randomised controlled trial. Participants will be MSM newly diagnosed with PHI (via recent negative test or antigen/RNA testing) who report chemsex in the past 3 months, recruited from sexual health clinics in England. The intervention arm will receive a tailored session based on the Behaviour Change Wheel, addressing risk environment, chemsex-related disinhibition, and U=U messaging, followed by a safetxt-style adaptive text message program for support and ART adherence. The control arm will receive standard care. Primary outcomes are the number of condomless anal sex acts with serodiscordant/unknown partners and time to ART initiation, measured via questionnaires and clinic data at 1 and 3 months. Analysis will use intention-to-treat with Poisson and Cox regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first RCT evidence on the effectiveness of a PHI-specific behavioural intervention for the high-priority chemsex-engaged MSM population. It will generate a manualised intervention package that can be integrated into sexual health pathways. Findings will directly inform public health strategies to interrupt transmission chains at their most infectious point.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely combines a focus on the acute PHI transmission window with the specific risk context of chemsex, applying a proven digital behavioural intervention method (safetxt) to a novel, high-stakes clinical scenario beyond STI reinfection.

proposals.title: Modelling the synergistic impact of PrEP and ART scale-up on HIV incidence among MSM in England: a dynamic transmission model incorporating chemsex-associated risk behaviours
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, HIV prevention among men who have sex with men (MSM) increasingly relies on a dual approach: antiretroviral therapy (ART) for those living with HIV and pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for those at risk. However, the population-level impact of their combined rollout is uncertain, particularly in the context of complex, high-risk behaviours like chemsex, which is associated with increased sexual risk-taking and suboptimal PrEP adherence. Understanding the synergistic effects of these interventions is critical for public health planning and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the combined scale-up of ART and PrEP will have a synergistic, greater-than-additive effect on reducing HIV incidence among MSM, but that this impact will be attenuated in sub-populations engaging in chemsex due to associated behavioural risk patterns and adherence challenges.
proposals.method: We will develop a deterministic, compartmental dynamic transmission model of HIV among MSM in England, stratified by chemsex engagement (based on AURAH2 study predictors), PrEP use, and ART status. The model will be parameterised using pre-2026 data from UK surveillance systems, the AURAH2 study, and published literature on PrEP/ART efficacy and adherence. We will use a system of ordinary differential equations to simulate scenarios of ART-only, PrEP-only, and combined scale-up over a 10-year horizon. The model will incorporate chemsex-associated increases in partner change rates and calibrated reductions in PrEP adherence. We will conduct uncertainty and sensitivity analyses using Latin Hypercube Sampling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first model-based estimate of the combined population-level prevention impact of ART and PrEP for MSM in England, explicitly quantifying the synergy. It will identify the degree to which chemsex-associated behaviours may undermine intervention effectiveness, offering evidence for targeted, multidimensional interventions to support this key population, as suggested by Hart's prior work on 'missing men'.
proposals.novelty: While models of ART or PrEP exist, few pre-2026 models integrate both interventions dynamically while also incorporating a specific, modifiable behavioural risk factor (chemsex) known to influence adherence and transmission networks, moving beyond homogeneous population assumptions.

proposals.title: A Randomised Controlled Trial of Two Messaging Frameworks on Stigma and Prevention Intentions Among MSM Engaged in Chemsex
proposals.motivation: Stigma surrounding sexually transmitted infections (STIs), particularly in contexts like chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM), remains a significant barrier to testing, treatment, and disclosure. Pre-2026 public health messaging often oscillates between emphasizing personal responsibility and promoting biomedical prevention (e.g., PrEP), but the differential impact of these frames on internalized and enacted stigma is poorly understood. This gap limits the effectiveness of interventions aiming to reduce STI incidence and improve sexual health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that messaging that frames STI prevention around shared community responsibility and normalizes infection as a manageable health outcome will result in lower levels of internalized stigma and higher intentions for regular testing compared to messaging that strongly emphasizes individual agency and personal fault.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-arm online randomised controlled trial (RCT) with approximately 900 MSM in England who report chemsex in the past year. Participants will be randomly assigned to view one of three short, professionally produced video messages: 1) a 'Personal Responsibility' frame, 2) a 'Normalization & Shared Prevention' frame, or 3) a neutral control video. Primary outcomes, measured immediately post-exposure and at 1-month follow-up, will be scores on validated scales for internalized STI stigma and intentions for quarterly STI testing. Analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to compare mean outcome scores between arms, adjusting for baseline characteristics (e.g., age, PrEP use, STI history).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence from an RCT on how specific public health message framing directly affects stigma in a high-risk population. It will offer concrete, evidence-based guidance for crafting destigmatizing communications within sexual health services and national campaigns. The findings will directly inform the development of behavioural interventions, like those in Dr. Hart's safetxt trial, by identifying optimal messaging content to reduce psychological barriers to prevention.
proposals.novelty: While previous research has described stigma correlates, this proposal experimentally tests the causal impact of competing, theory-driven message frames used in real-world health promotion. It moves beyond documenting associations to actively evaluating a modifiable intervention point (messaging) at the intersection of chemsex, PrEP use, and STI management.

proposals.title: Adapting the 'Time to Change' mental health anti-stigma framework to reduce chlamydia-related stigma and increase testing in young people: a mixed-methods study and pilot randomised controlled trial.
proposals.motivation: Chlamydia is highly prevalent but often asymptomatic, and stigma is a documented barrier to testing and treatment, particularly among young people and sexual minorities. Pre-2026 evidence shows that stigma reduction campaigns in mental health (e.g., 'Time to Change') have been effective in changing public attitudes and help-seeking behaviours. Applying these proven frameworks to a sexual health context represents a novel, theory-driven approach to a persistent public health problem.
proposals.hypothesis: That a chlamydia destigmatization intervention, co-designed with young people and based on core components of evidence-based mental health anti-stigma campaigns (e.g., social contact, myth-busting), will significantly reduce internalised and anticipated stigma and increase intentions to seek testing compared to standard health promotion materials.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-phase study. Phase 1: Qualitative interviews and focus groups with young people (aged 16-24) to adapt the 'Time to Change' framework components (contact, education, protest) to chlamydia. Phase 2: Co-design workshops to create prototype destigmatization materials (e.g., short videos, infographics). Phase 3: A pilot randomised controlled trial (RCT) where participants are randomised to view the novel materials or standard Public Health England information. Primary outcomes will be changes in validated stigma scales and testing intentions, analysed using linear regression models, with process evaluation to assess feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first theoretically grounded, co-designed intervention package specifically targeting chlamydia stigma. It will provide preliminary efficacy data on a novel application of mental health frameworks to sexual health, and a manualised protocol for a future definitive RCT. The findings will directly inform public health messaging and clinic-based interventions to reduce barriers to STI testing.
proposals.novelty: While stigma is recognised as a barrier in sexual health, interventions have rarely been explicitly built on frameworks with proven efficacy in other health domains. This study innovatively transplants and adapts the core, evidence-based architecture of a successful mental health anti-stigma campaign to address chlamydia, moving beyond awareness-raising to targeted attitude and behaviour change.

proposals.title: The psychosocial impact of sexually transmitted infection (STI) and HIV PrEP screening on men who have sex with men (MSM) in England: a qualitative study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, public health research on MSM often focuses on biomedical outcomes (e.g., STI incidence) and behavioural risk factors, particularly in contexts like chemsex. However, the psychosocial impact of routine screening—a cornerstone of prevention for MSM using PrEP or engaging in chemsex—remains underexplored. Understanding this impact is crucial, as screening-related anxiety, stigma, or disruption could influence future testing adherence and engagement with care, potentially undermining prevention efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that for MSM, particularly those engaged in chemsex or using PrEP, routine STI/HIV screening generates significant, multi-faceted psychosocial impacts (e.g., anxiety, shame, relationship strain) that are not adequately addressed by current clinical pathways, and that these impacts vary by social context and substance use patterns.
proposals.method: We will conduct a qualitative interview study with 30-40 MSM in England recruited via sexual health clinics and community-based organisations, oversampling for PrEP users and those reporting recent chemsex. Using a semi-structured interview guide, we will explore experiences of recent screening, including emotional responses, perceived stigma, and impact on identity and sexual behaviours. Interviews will be audio-recorded, transcribed, and analysed using a combination of inductive thematic analysis and framework analysis, informed by Hart's prior work on predictors of chemsex and the 'Missing men' paper's emphasis on structural influences on health-seeking.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first in-depth qualitative evidence on the unintended psychosocial consequences of routine screening for MSM in England, complementing existing quantitative trial data (e.g., safetxt, AURAH2). It will generate actionable insights for clinics to develop more supportive, person-centred screening services that mitigate negative impacts, thereby potentially improving long-term engagement in prevention.
proposals.novelty: While Hart's previous work identified predictors of chemsex and evaluated behavioural interventions, this study pivots to examine the clinical prevention interface itself from a male psychosocial perspective, a gap highlighted in the 'Missing men' paper but not yet applied to sexual health screening in high-income settings.

proposals.title: Assessing the Reliability of Self-Reported Sexual Behaviour in Chemsex and PrEP Studies: A Validation Study Using Biomarker and Administrative Data Linkage
proposals.motivation: Research on chemsex and PrEP adherence, crucial for HIV/STI prevention, relies heavily on self-reported sexual behaviour and drug use, which are susceptible to social desirability and recall bias. This undermines the validity of risk assessments and intervention outcomes, as seen in trials like safetxt and cohort studies like AURAH2. Establishing the degree of measurement error is essential to improve the evidence base for behavioural public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Self-reported measures of condomless sex and chemsex engagement among men who have sex with men (MSM) on PrEP will show significant discrepancies when validated against objective biomarkers for sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and linked pharmacy records of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) prescriptions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study recruiting 500 MSM on PrEP in England. Participants will complete quarterly surveys on sexual behaviour and chemsex, mirroring AURAH2. Objective validation will involve: 1) Linking self-reported condomless sex to quarterly STI testing results (chlamydia, gonorrhoea) as a biomarker of risk. 2) Linking self-reported chemsex episodes and associated sexual risk to real-time administrative data on PEP prescriptions from NHS systems. We will use generalized estimating equations (GEE) to model the probability of an objective risk event (STI+ or PEP prescription) as a function of self-reported behaviour, quantifying sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide quantitative estimates of measurement error for key behavioural outcomes in sexual health research. It will directly inform the design and interpretation of future trials and cohort studies by indicating where self-report is sufficiently reliable and where supplementary objective measures are critical. The findings will strengthen methodological rigor in the field of behavioural HIV prevention.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of self-report are known, this study innovatively combines prospective behavioural data with two distinct streams of objective data (biomarkers and real-world administrative prescriptions) to provide a multi-faceted validation specifically within the high-priority context of chemsex and PrEP use.

proposals.title: Platform-Specific Risk Profiles: A Cross-Sectional Study of HIV Status, Chemsex, and PrEP Use Among Men Who Have Sex with Men Recruited from Geosocial Networking and Chat Room Platforms
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work on chemsex predictors, shows that sexual networking platforms are central to the HIV prevention landscape for men who have sex with men (MSM). However, it is unclear if risk profiles—including HIV status, chemsex engagement, and PrEP use—are homogenously distributed or if they cluster on specific platforms. Understanding this is critical for targeting public health interventions effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct geosocial networking apps (e.g., Grindr, Scruff) and online chat rooms/websites (e.g., recon, specific fetish forums) will attract MSM with significantly different profiles of HIV status, recent chemsex behavior, and current PrEP use.
proposals.method: We will conduct an online cross-sectional survey of MSM in England, recruiting approximately 1,500 participants via targeted advertisements on Grindr, Scruff, Hornet, and specific chat room platforms. The survey will assess self-reported HIV status, PrEP use, chemsex behavior (defined as sex under the influence of specific drugs), and sexual risk behaviors. We will use multinomial logistic regression to model the primary recruitment platform as the outcome, adjusting for demographic confounders, to identify platform-specific associations with the key risk variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative comparison of HIV-related risk profiles across specific online platforms used by MSM in the UK. It will directly inform the digital targeting of health promotion messages, PrEP information, and chemsex harm reduction services, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' online approach. The findings will extend Dr. Hart's trial expertise into a novel, platform-based behavioral epidemiology context.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has studied online-recruited MSM as a single group or compared online to offline recruitment, this study innovates by comparing risk profiles *between* specific, commonly used digital platforms, treating the platform ecology as a key structural determinant of risk clustering.

proposals.title: Mapping HIV/STI transmission potential in online-offline partner networks of MSM in England: a social network analysis of the AURAH2 cohort
proposals.motivation: Chemsex and online partner-seeking significantly shape sexual networks among men who have sex with men (MSM), creating dense clusters and long-distance ties that can facilitate rapid HIV/STI transmission. Pre-2026, while studies like AURAH2 identify individual-level risk factors, the structural pathways of transmission through these hybrid online-offline networks remain poorly mapped. Understanding these network topologies is critical for designing targeted interventions that go beyond individual behaviour change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that online-offline sexual networks among MSM, particularly those engaging in chemsex, exhibit structural features (e.g., high clustering, short path lengths, and the presence of high-degree 'bridge' individuals) that significantly increase the potential population-level transmission efficiency of HIV and bacterial STIs compared to networks formed primarily through offline means.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the AURAH2 prospective study data, using participant-reported sexual partner data (including meeting venue: online app/offline) to reconstruct egocentric networks and infer a larger connected component. We will apply social network analysis (SNA) methods, calculating metrics like degree centrality, betweenness centrality, clustering coefficient, and average path length. Exponential random graph models (ERGMs) will be used to test the significance of network features associated with chemsex engagement and STI incidence, modelling the potential transmission pathways for specific infections.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of potential HIV/STI transmission pathways within the hybrid online-offline networks of a UK MSM cohort, quantifying the role of network structure. It will directly inform the development of structural interventions, such as network-based PrEP and testing strategies, by identifying critical points (e.g., highly connected individuals or bridging ties) for maximising public health impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hart et al. has examined predictors of chemsex initiation, this proposal innovatively applies formal SNA to model the *transmission consequences* of these behavioural patterns. It moves beyond individual risk to model population-level transmission dynamics arising from the intersection of chemsex, online partner-seeking, and PrEP use, a synthesis not yet addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Linguistic Markers of HIV Status Disclosure and Serosorting in Online Dating Profiles of Men Who Have Sex with Men: A Text-Mining Analysis
proposals.motivation: Online dating platforms are a primary venue for sexual partner seeking among men who have sex with men (MSM), influencing HIV prevention dynamics like serosorting. Understanding how HIV status is communicated or inferred in profile text is crucial for designing effective digital health interventions. Pre-2026, research on chemsex and PrEP use in MSM, such as Hart's AURAH2 work, highlights the need to examine how online behaviours intersect with sexual health risks and protective strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific linguistic features and thematic clusters within dating profile text (e.g., mentions of PrEP, undetectable, specific drug terms, or sexual health language) are significantly associated with explicit HIV status disclosure and implicit markers of serosorting behaviour.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of public dating profile text from MSM-focused platforms (e.g., Scruff, Grindr via public API where permissible pre-2026). Using natural language processing, we will apply a supervised machine learning pipeline: 1) Feature extraction using pre-trained word embeddings (e.g., GloVe) and topic modeling (Latent Dirichlet Allocation), 2) Training of classifier models (e.g., Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines) to predict profiles with explicit status disclosure, using manually coded profiles as ground truth. We will then identify key n-grams and topics associated with disclosure and correlate these with language indicative of serosorting preferences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, data-driven map of how HIV status and serosorting intentions are linguistically constructed in online MSM communities. It will extend behavioural intervention science (cf. Hart's safetxt trial) by identifying novel digital markers for targeted outreach. Findings will inform the development of nuanced, language-aware public health messaging and app-based interventions to support safer sexual decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While previous research surveys MSM about online behaviours, this study directly analyzes the naturalistic text data from profiles themselves using computational linguistics. It moves beyond self-report to uncover implicit, observable linguistic patterns associated with disclosure and sorting, a method not yet applied in Hart's published work on chemsex predictors or TB interventions.

proposals.title: Quantifying Partner Preference Trade-offs: A Discrete Choice Experiment on HIV Status, PrEP Use, and Setting Among Men Who Have Sex with Men in England
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 era of widespread PrEP availability and the rise of chemsex, understanding HIV risk perception and partner selection is crucial for effective prevention. Prior research by Hart et al. has linked chemsex to sexual risk behaviours and STI incidence, but preferences for partner HIV status in different settings remain unquantified. This gap limits the ability to design targeted interventions that address the complex interplay of biomedical prevention (PrEP), substance use, and partner-seeking contexts (online apps vs. offline venues).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative importance (utility) of a potential partner's HIV status for MSM is significantly moderated by the setting (online vs. offline) and the respondent's own PrEP use and recent chemsex engagement, with PrEP users demonstrating a lower marginal utility for an HIV-negative status compared to non-users.
proposals.method: We will design a web-based discrete choice experiment (DCE) embedded within a cross-sectional survey of MSM in England (target N=800). Attributes will include partner HIV status (negative/undetectable/unknown), PrEP use, chemsex interest, and attractiveness. Using a D-efficient experimental design in Ngene software, participants will complete 12 choice tasks selecting between two hypothetical partner profiles. Data will be analyzed using mixed logit models in Stata to estimate preference weights and calculate willingness-to-accept trade-offs, testing for interactions with setting, personal PrEP use, and chemsex behaviour.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of how MSM trade off partner HIV status against other attributes in different settings, directly informing risk communication and partner notification strategies. It will extend Hart's work on chemsex predictors and behavioural trials by applying a rigorous economic method to measure preferences, offering novel evidence for structural interventions aimed at reducing stigma and improving sexual health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While DCEs are established in health economics, their application to model real-time sexual decision-making in the context of chemsex and PrEP is novel. This moves beyond documenting associations of risk to quantifying the decision calculus, offering a new lens on the 'missing men' problem in HIV/STI prevention by revealing preference structures that may drive partner selection.

proposals.title: Understanding the 'Why': A Qualitative Exploration of Motivations for Chemsex Among Men Who Have Sex with Men in England to Inform Harm Reduction and PrEP Engagement
proposals.motivation: Chemsex (using drugs like mephedrone, GHB/GBL, and crystal methamphetamine to facilitate sex) is associated with significant health risks, including HIV/STI transmission, mental health crises, and overdose, among men who have sex with men (MSM). While quantitative studies, including Dr. Hart's prior work in AURAH2, have identified predictors and prevalence, the underlying motivations remain poorly understood, limiting the development of effective, targeted behavioural and clinical interventions, particularly in the context of widespread PrEP availability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that motivations for chemsex are multidimensional, extending beyond sexual enhancement to include coping with minority stress, facilitating social/community connection, and managing the psychological demands of HIV/PrEP management, and that these motivations cluster in ways that predict differential risk profiles and intervention needs.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 25-30 MSM in England who report chemsex in the past year, recruited via sexual health clinics and community-based organisations. Interviews will explore decision-making contexts, perceived benefits, and links to sexual health practices. Using a hybrid thematic analysis approach, data will be coded inductively and deductively using a framework informed by the Integrated Theoretical Model of Sexual Risk Behaviour. Analysis will identify core motivational themes and their interrelationships.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, participant-driven taxonomy of chemsex motivations, moving beyond risk-centric narratives. It will directly generate actionable insights for tailoring existing interventions (e.g., safetxt-style messaging, clinical consultations) to address the specific drivers of behaviour, thereby improving the relevance and effectiveness of harm reduction and PrEP support services for this population.
proposals.novelty: This work integrates qualitative depth with Dr. Hart's established quantitative trajectory on chemsex, applying a behavioural theory lens specifically to motivation—a gap in pre-2026 literature which predominantly focuses on correlates and outcomes rather than the lived experiential 'why'.

proposals.title: Testing Niche Conservatism in Chemsex Behaviours: A Longitudinal Study of Behavioural Stability Following HIV Seroconversion Among MSM in England
proposals.motivation: The widespread availability of PrEP and U=U has profoundly altered the HIV risk calculus, potentially changing the relationship between HIV status and sexualised drug use (chemsex). Pre-2026, most research treats HIV-negative status as a stable behavioural category, but this may be an oversimplification. Understanding whether chemsex behaviours are fundamentally defined by HIV status or are stable traits with other drivers is critical for designing effective, status-neutral harm reduction interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemsex behaviours will demonstrate niche conservatism; that is, patterns of drug use (frequency, type, context) will remain stable following HIV seroconversion, indicating they are part of a 'fundamental behavioural niche' shaped by social and structural factors, rather than a 'realized niche' primarily constrained by the fear of acquiring HIV.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal behavioural data from the AURAH2 study, identifying a cohort of MSM who seroconverted during follow-up. Using a pre-post, within-subjects design, we will model changes in chemsex outcomes (initiation, cessation, frequency) using generalized estimating equations (GEE) to account for repeated measures. We will adapt ecological niche modelling concepts by operationalizing the 'pre-seroconversion niche' (realized) and comparing it to the 'post-seroconversion niche', testing for stability (conservatism) against a counterfactual of significant change.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, theory-driven test of behavioural plasticity in the PrEP/U=U era, moving beyond correlational studies. It will generate evidence on whether HIV prevention messaging should be status-specific or integrated. The application of an ecological framework offers a new lens for understanding the stability of complex health behaviours in response to a major biomedical life event.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in reframing seroconversion as a natural experiment to test behavioural theory, rather than solely as a clinical outcome. It explicitly challenges the assumed primacy of HIV status as a behavioural determinant by importing and adapting the well-established ecological concepts of fundamental/realized niche and niche conservatism.

proposals.title: Chemsex Trajectories and HIV Seroconversion: A Prospective Cohort Study of Drug Use Patterns Before and After Diagnosis in Men Who Have Sex with Men in England
proposals.motivation: Chemsex (using drugs to facilitate sex) is a significant risk factor for HIV acquisition among men who have sex with men (MSM). Pre-2026 research, including Hart's work, has focused on predictors of starting or stopping chemsex, but the impact of an HIV diagnosis on subsequent drug use patterns is poorly understood. This gap is critical for designing effective, targeted harm reduction and support interventions for individuals navigating a new HIV diagnosis, especially in the context of widespread PrEP availability which may have previously influenced risk perceptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that HIV seroconversion acts as a catalyst for a significant change in chemsex-related drug use patterns, with distinct trajectories of increased, decreased, or stable use post-diagnosis, and that these trajectories are associated with specific psychosocial and behavioural factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study nested within an existing sexual health and HIV surveillance framework (e.g., AURAH studies). We will recruit HIV-negative MSM reporting recent chemsex, with baseline behavioural and psychosocial assessments. Participants will be followed quarterly via electronic surveys and annual STI/HIV testing. Those who seroconvert (cases) will be matched to controls who remain HIV-negative. Using group-based trajectory modelling (GBTM), we will identify distinct longitudinal patterns of chemsex frequency and drug types used in the 12 months pre- and post-seroconversion. Multinomial logistic regression will analyse associations between trajectory group membership and factors like PrEP use, mental health, and sexual behaviours.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence on how chemsex behaviours evolve following an HIV diagnosis, moving beyond cross-sectional associations. It will identify modifiable factors associated with harmful versus adaptive behavioural trajectories, informing the development of timely, post-diagnosis behavioural interventions. The findings will directly contribute to integrated care models that address both HIV and substance use.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines predictors of chemsex, this study uniquely conceptualises HIV seroconversion as a potential turning point, analysing dynamic behavioural change rather than static risk factors. It applies advanced longitudinal modelling (GBTM) to a critical, understudied period in the HIV care continuum.

proposals.title: The Host-Environment Nexus: Testing a Syndemic Model of Harm in Chemsex Among Men Who Have Sex with Men
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, linked to sexual risk, poor mental health, and drug-related harms. Pre-2026 research and interventions often focus on the pharmacological properties of specific drugs (e.g., mephedrone, GHB/GBL) as the primary drivers of harm. This substance-centric 'vector' model may overlook critical psychological and social determinants, limiting the effectiveness of prevention and support strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that syndemic factors—specifically, pre-existing depression/anxiety (host susceptibility) and social isolation/lack of gay community connectedness (environmental context)—will be stronger predictors of chemsex-related harm (e.g., overdose, sexual assault, PrEP discontinuation, STI acquisition) than the specific type or frequency of chemsex drug use.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal cohort data (e.g., AURAH2, linking to Hart's prior work) using multivariate logistic regression to model the odds of harm. Predictors will include drug type/frequency, mental health scores (e.g., PHQ-9, GAD-7), and validated measures of social isolation and community attachment. We will then use structural equation modelling (SEM) to test a theoretical path model where mental health and social environment have direct and indirect effects on harm, mediating or moderating the role of specific substances.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence to shift the conceptual framework for chemsex harm from a purely pharmacological to a syndemic, person-in-context model. It will identify modifiable psychosocial targets for intervention, complementing existing substance-focused approaches. The findings could directly inform the design of behavioural interventions, like text-message supports (cf. safetxt trial), tailored to underlying mental health and social needs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates chemsex with mental health and risk, this study directly tests an epidemiological 'host-environment' analogy against the 'drug-as-vector' model. It quantitatively compares the predictive power of these competing frameworks to determine which better explains variance in harm, a novel approach in chemsex research.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways in Chemsex: A Biologically-Validated Cohort Study to Disentangle Direct Drug Effects from Psychosocial Mediators on STI Risk
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, strongly associated with increased risk of HIV and STIs. Pre-2026 research, including Hart's work, relies heavily on self-reported data, creating uncertainty about the true causal pathways: whether specific drugs directly increase risk-taking, or whether underlying psychosocial vulnerabilities drive both drug use and risk. Resolving this is critical for designing effective, targeted harm-reduction interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary drivers of STI risk in chemsex are not the direct pharmacological effects of specific drugs, but rather pre-existing psychosocial factors (e.g., depression, social isolation, sexual compulsivity), with drug use acting as a mediator or a behavioural outcome of these conditions.
proposals.method: We will recruit a prospective cohort of 500 MSM reporting chemsex in London. At quarterly visits, participants will complete detailed psychosocial surveys and provide biological samples for STI testing (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis) and hair/toxicology analysis to objectively quantify drug exposure. Using this biologically-validated dataset, we will employ Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) and Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to test competing causal models. Advanced mediation analysis will quantify the proportion of STI risk attributable to direct drug effects versus indirect pathways through psychosocial mediators.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first high-fidelity, biologically-validated dataset for causal inference in chemsex research. It will directly identify the most potent intervention targets (e.g., specific drugs vs. mental health support) by quantifying causal pathways. The findings will inform the development of precise behavioural or combination interventions to reduce harm in this population.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond correlational studies by integrating objective biological measures of both outcome (STI) and exposure (drug toxicology) to strengthen causal claims. It directly tests competing theoretical models of chemsex risk, a methodologically rigorous approach not yet applied in this field.

proposals.title: Understanding the 'Why': A Cognitive Interviewing Study of Sexual Health Information Comprehension Among Men Who Have Sex with Men in Non-Metropolitan England
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work on chemsex and PrEP, highlights significant sexual health disparities for MSM. However, most studies and interventions are developed and trialled in major urban centres. MSM in smaller cities and rural areas face distinct challenges, including potential isolation, less visible community networks, and services not tailored to their context, which may critically impact how they understand health information, access services, and manage risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MSM in non-metropolitan settings will demonstrate systematic differences in their comprehension and interpretation of key sexual health terms (e.g., 'chemsex', 'PrEP', 'undetectable'), service pathways, and public health messaging compared to urban-centric understandings, and that these differences are shaped by localized social environments and resource availability.
proposals.method: We will employ cognitive interviewing, a qualitative survey methodology, with a purposive sample of 25-30 MSM recruited via non-metropolitan sexual health services and online platforms outside London. Participants will complete a short survey containing selected terms and messages from national campaigns (e.g., from the 'It Starts With Me' campaign) and local service descriptions. Using concurrent verbal probing techniques (e.g., paraphrasing, confidence ratings, specific probes on terms like 'STI clinic confidentiality'), we will elicit their thought processes, interpretations, and potential misunderstandings. Interviews will be audio-recorded, transcribed, and analysed using thematic analysis to identify patterns of comprehension and their contextual drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a nuanced, evidence-based map of comprehension gaps and their socio-geographic roots for a key underserved population. The findings will directly inform the tailoring of health communications, service descriptions, and potentially future behavioural interventions (like safetxt) for non-metropolitan MSM, aiming to improve health literacy, service engagement, and ultimately health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While cognitive interviewing is established in health literacy research, its application is novel for investigating the specific health information needs of non-metropolitan MSM in the UK, moving beyond descriptive epidemiology to uncover the cognitive mechanisms behind comprehension shaped by local context.

proposals.title: Longitudinal trajectories of chemsex engagement and PrEP adherence among GBMSM in England: a mixed-methods cohort study of psychological, social, and network drivers
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM) is associated with sexual risk behaviours and STI acquisition. The widespread introduction of PrEP has transformed HIV prevention but its complex, bidirectional relationship with chemsex—where PrEP may enable or be disrupted by drug use—is poorly understood over time. Prospective, mechanistic studies are needed to inform integrated harm reduction and sexual health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Distinct longitudinal trajectories of chemsex engagement (starting, stopping, persisting) will be differentially associated with patterns of PrEP adherence and sexual health outcomes, mediated by psychological drivers (e.g., intentionality, mental health) and social network characteristics.
proposals.method: We will recruit four prospectively followed cohorts of GBMSM in England (N=800) based on baseline PrEP use and chemsex engagement. Annual quantitative surveys over 3 years will measure chemsex practices, PrEP adherence, STI incidence, mental health, and intentionality. Embedded qualitative interviews (n=80) and egocentric social network mapping at baseline and year 2 will provide context. Analysis will use group-based trajectory modelling to identify chemsex/PrEP pathways, with time-lagged generalised estimating equations to test associations with outcomes, controlling for confounders like age and ethnicity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed mapping of co-evolving chemsex and PrEP trajectories, identifying modifiable psychological and social mediators. It will generate evidence on whether chemsex primarily disrupts PrEP use or coexists with high adherence, informing targeted behavioural interventions and clinical guidelines for GBMSM engaging in chemsex.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond cross-sectional 'risk factor' studies by prospectively modelling trajectories and integrating quantitative, qualitative, and social network data to uncover the mechanisms and intentionality behind changes in practice, a methodologically advanced approach not yet applied to this specific question.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Behavioural Cascade Model of PrEP Adoption Among Men Who Have Sex with Men Engaging in Chemsex: The ChemPrep Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is associated with increased HIV risk behaviours, yet PrEP uptake and adherence in this group remain suboptimal and poorly understood. Existing models often treat chemsex as a static risk factor, failing to capture the dynamic interplay between evolving drug-use patterns, risk perception, and prevention decisions over time. This limits the development of timely, effective interventions.
