proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PrEP adoption and sustained use among MSM who engage in chemsex follows a non-linear, cascading process, where shifts in the perceived severity of HIV risk during chemsex episodes, combined with changes in PrEP access and evolving peer network norms, interact to predict critical transitions in prevention behaviour.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month prospective cohort study of 500 HIV-negative MSM reporting recent chemsex in England, with surveys at baseline, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. We will adapt the longitudinal behavioural cascade model from climate adaptation science (e.g., the 'socio-cognitive process model' of protective action). Using structural equation modelling (SEM) with cross-lagged panels, we will operationalize the cascade: stressor perception (HIV risk appraisal during chemsex), solution access (PrEP prescription/access barriers), and social norms (descriptive/injunctive norms within sexual/drug-using networks). Latent transition analysis will identify distinct trajectories of PrEP use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dynamic, process-oriented model of PrEP behaviour in the context of chemsex, moving beyond static correlates. It will identify critical, time-sensitive intervention points (e.g., when risk perception shifts) to promote PrEP initiation and prevent discontinuation. Findings will directly inform the design of adaptive, longitudinal behavioural interventions tailored to this high-priority population.
proposals.novelty: The novel adaptation of a behavioural cascade framework from environmental science to chemsex/PrEP dynamics represents a significant conceptual leap. It explicitly models the temporal interdependencies between risk environment, access, and norms—a non-obvious integration given that existing HIV behavioural research rarely employs such dynamic systems models from other fields.

proposals.title: Trajectories of Chemsex and PrEP Engagement: A 24-Month Prospective Cohort Study of Men Who Have Sex with Men in England
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is associated with high-risk sexual behaviours and increased HIV risk. Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) is a highly effective HIV prevention tool, but the dynamic relationship between chemsex participation and PrEP adoption, adherence, and discontinuation over time is poorly understood. Understanding these longitudinal patterns is critical for designing timely and effective public health and harm reduction interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that distinct, identifiable trajectories of chemsex engagement (e.g., initiation, persistence, cessation) will be differentially associated with patterns of PrEP uptake, consistent adherence, and discontinuation over a 24-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month prospective cohort study of 800 HIV-negative MSM in England, recruited via sexual health clinics and community-based settings. Participants will complete detailed behavioural surveys at baseline, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months, assessing chemsex drug use (specific substances, frequency, context), PrEP use (prescription, adherence, gaps), and sexual behaviours. We will use group-based trajectory modelling (GBTM) to identify distinct longitudinal patterns of chemsex engagement. Multivariable logistic and multinomial regression, with time-lagged analyses, will then model associations between these trajectory groups and subsequent PrEP outcomes, adjusting for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed longitudinal mapping of how chemsex and PrEP behaviours co-evolve, identifying critical timepoints for intervention. It will inform targeted strategies to support PrEP adherence among those who persist with chemsex and promote harm reduction for those initiating or ceasing chemsex, thereby optimising HIV prevention efforts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined cross-sectional associations, this study applies advanced longitudinal modelling (GBTM) to characterise *trajectories* of chemsex, moving beyond static 'user/non-user' categorisations to dynamically model their causal and temporal links with PrEP behaviours over time.

proposals.title: Mapping Structural Disincentives: A Mixed-Methods Study of Health System Barriers to STI Prevention for Men Who Have Sex with Men Engaged in Chemsex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, robustly links chemsex among MSM with increased STI incidence and identifies individual-level risk factors. However, the role of health system structures in exacerbating this risk remains underexplored. This is critical because bacterial STI rates remain high, and systemic barriers may undermine the effectiveness of individual-level interventions like PrEP and behavioural messaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, modifiable features of sexual health service design and delivery—including clinic accessibility, provider communication, and care integration—act as structural disincentives, reducing timely STI testing and treatment engagement for MSM practicing chemsex, independent of individual risk behaviours.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent parallel mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will analyse linked clinical and administrative data from sexual health clinics in England (e.g., GUMCADV2) to model associations between service characteristics (e.g., opening hours, waiting times) and STI testing/re-testing intervals for MSM, stratified by drug-use markers. Qualitatively, we will conduct in-depth interviews and focus groups with MSM with recent chemsex experience and healthcare providers, using thematic analysis to identify perceived barriers. Data will be integrated to triangulate findings and specify actionable system flaws.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from documenting individual risk to identifying concrete, systemic intervention points. It will produce an evidence-based framework of structural barriers to inform service redesign, aiming to make STI prevention more accessible and appropriate. The findings will directly support the development of targeted, structural interventions to complement existing behavioural and biomedical approaches.
proposals.novelty: While existing work correlates chemsex with STIs, this study uniquely applies a health systems lens to this issue, treating service design as an independent variable affecting outcomes. It synthesises Dr. Hart's expertise in chemsex, trial methodology, and structural interventions for tuberculosis into a novel investigation of sexual health systems.

proposals.title: Estimating the Prevalence and Correlates of Polydrug Use and Chemsex Among MSM Not Attending Sexual Health Clinics: A Respondent-Driven Sampling Study in England
proposals.motivation: Chemsex and polydrug use among men who have sex with men (MSM) are associated with significant health risks, including HIV/STI transmission and mental health harms. Existing prevalence data are primarily derived from clinic-based samples, which systematically miss MSM who do not access these services, leading to biased estimates and hindering targeted public health interventions. Understanding the behaviors and needs of this hidden subgroup is critical for effective health promotion and harm reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Respondent-Driven Sampling (RDS) can generate a statistically valid sample of non-clinic-attending MSM, and that the population-weighted prevalence of polydrug use and chemsex in this group will be significantly different from estimates derived from clinic-based samples.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard RDS protocol, recruiting 8-10 diverse initial 'seeds' from non-clinic venues (e.g., geosocial networking apps, community events). Each participant completes a confidential survey on drug use, sexual behaviors, and PrEP use, and receives 3-5 uniquely coded coupons to recruit peers from their social network. Recruitment continues until equilibrium and sample size targets are met. Data will be analyzed using RDS Analyst software (RDS-II estimator) to generate population prevalence estimates and logistic regression models with RDS-adjusted weights to identify associated behavioral and demographic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-representative prevalence estimates of polydrug use and chemsex among a critical but hidden subgroup of MSM in England. It will directly inform the targeting of outreach, harm reduction messaging, and clinical service provision. The adapted methodology offers a blueprint for future surveillance of hard-to-reach populations within sexual health research.
proposals.novelty: While RDS is established for people who inject drugs, its systematic application to sample non-clinic-attending MSM for polydrug use assessment is novel. This approach directly addresses the 'missing men' problem in sexual health epidemiology by moving beyond clinic-based convenience sampling.

proposals.title: Simulating the Feedback Loop: An Agent-Based Model of HIV Seroconversion and Evolving Chemsex Behaviours to Evaluate Intervention Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant driver of HIV transmission and STI acquisition. While PrEP is highly effective, its real-world impact is mediated by complex, dynamic behaviours. Current models often treat HIV status and associated risk behaviours as static, failing to capture how a seroconversion event can fundamentally alter an individual's drug use, sexual networking, and engagement with services, creating feedback loops that affect population-level intervention outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the population-level effectiveness of chemsex-related interventions (e.g., PrEP, peer support) is significantly modulated by post-seroconversion behavioural changes, and that agent-based modeling which incorporates these dynamic trajectories will reveal non-linear, network-dependent intervention impacts not apparent in static models.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-cohort, dynamic agent-based model (ABM) using a modified version of the classic susceptible-infected (SI) framework within a sexual network. Agents will be initialized with behavioural parameters (chemsex frequency, PrEP use, network degree) informed by Dr. Hart's AURAH2 data and other pre-2026 MSM cohort studies. Core algorithms will include: 1) a stochastic HIV transmission module weighted by condomless sex and viral load, 2) behavioural transition rules where seroconversion probabilistically triggers changes in chemsex practice (e.g., cessation or intensification) and service engagement, informed by qualitative literature, and 3) intervention modules (e.g., targeted PrEP promotion, peer navigation) that agents can adopt or disengage from over time. The model will be implemented in NetLogo or a similar ABM platform.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel computational tool to simulate the long-term, system-level consequences of interventions within chemsex communities, accounting for behavioural evolution. It will generate evidence on which intervention combinations (biomedical and behavioural) are most resilient to the dynamic nature of risk, directly informing the design of targeted public health strategies for this high-priority population.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the explicit, data-driven modeling of behavioural trajectories *after* HIV seroconversion as a dynamic feedback mechanism within a sexual network ABM, moving beyond the standard assumption of fixed risk profiles to evaluate how interventions perform in a realistically evolving population.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Syndemic: A Structural Equation Model of Chemsex, Mental Health, and Sexual Risk in MSM on PrEP
proposals.motivation: Syndemic theory posits that substance use, mental ill-health, and sexual risk behaviours synergistically interact to worsen health outcomes, particularly among marginalised groups like men who have sex with men (MSM). Pre-2026, qualitative and epidemiological studies have described these co-occurring epidemics, but quantitative tests of the proposed synergistic pathways are lacking. This gap limits the development of targeted, integrated interventions for populations like MSM engaged in chemsex, who are increasingly using PrEP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that a syndemic model, where latent constructs of psychological distress and chemsex engagement have a bidirectional relationship, will provide a significantly better fit to observed data on sexual risk outcomes (e.g., condomless sex, STI incidence) than models treating these factors as independent or merely additive.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal data from the AURAH2 study, employing Structural Equation Modeling (SEM). The analysis will specify latent variables for psychological distress (using validated scales) and chemsex engagement (frequency, poly-drug use). We will test a cross-lagged panel model to assess bidirectional temporal relationships and their combined effect on a latent sexual risk behaviour variable. Model fit will be evaluated using Comparative Fit Index (CFI), Tucker-Lewis Index (TLI), and Root Mean Square Error of Approximation (RMSEA), comparing the syndemic model to alternative nested models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first quantitative, model-based tests of syndemic theory in the context of chemsex, moving beyond descriptive association. It will identify specific, modifiable pathways linking mental health and drug use to sexual risk, informing the design of multidimensional behavioural interventions. The findings will directly extend Dr. Hart's work on predictors of chemsex and text-message interventions (safetxt) by providing a mechanistic framework.
proposals.novelty: While syndemic theory is widely cited, its central tenet of synergistic interaction is rarely tested with advanced quantitative methods like SEM in this population. Applying a rigorous, hypothesis-driven modeling approach to high-quality cohort data represents a significant methodological advance over prior correlational studies.

proposals.title: Inverse Inference of Chemsex Drug Use from Sexual Risk Behaviour Patterns: A Machine Learning Analysis of the AURAH2 Cohort
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, associated with HIV/STI transmission risk and harm. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, typically models risk behaviours as outcomes of drug use. The inverse problem—using observable sexual risk patterns to infer likely, often undisclosed, drug use—is under-explored but critical for targeted, low-threshold interventions where direct drug use reporting is unreliable.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific constellations of sexual risk behaviours (e.g., partner numbers, condomless sex, PrEP use, STI history) can be used to probabilistically infer engagement in chemsex and the likely use of specific drugs (e.g., mephedrone, GHB/GBL, crystal methamphetamine) with significant accuracy.
proposals.method: We will analyse longitudinal data from the AURAH2 prospective study. Using baseline and follow-up data on sexual behaviours and reported drug use, we will train supervised machine learning models. The primary approach will be a multi-task, multi-label classification framework (e.g., using scikit-learn or XGBoost) to model the likelihood of using each drug. We will employ feature engineering on behavioural sequences and use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values for model interpretability, identifying the most predictive behavioural patterns for each substance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, behaviour-based predictive tool for identifying MSM likely engaged in chemsex, complementing traditional self-report. It will contribute methodologically by demonstrating an 'inverse' modelling approach in behavioural epidemiology. Findings will inform the design of more precise, data-driven digital (e.g., text message) or clinic-based interventions aimed at reducing drug-related harm.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines drug use as a predictor of risk, this study uniquely inverts the causal direction for predictive modelling. It moves beyond simple association to create a practical inference tool, integrating Dr. Hart's expertise in behavioural trials (safetxt) and longitudinal analysis (AURAH2) with interpretable machine learning techniques.

proposals.title: Real-time Contextual Triggers: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Chemsex, PrEP Adherence, and STI Incidence among Men who have Sex with Men
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among MSM is associated with increased HIV/STI transmission risk and potential impacts on PrEP adherence, but existing longitudinal studies lack granular, real-time data on the situational mechanisms. Understanding the immediate affective, social, and pharmacological contexts that link substance use to sexual risk and clinical outcomes is critical for developing effective, timely interventions. This addresses a key gap in the syndemic of substance use, sexual health, and HIV in MSM populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, time-varying contextual factors (e.g., negative affect, specific drug combinations, partner type) will mediate the relationship between chemsex episodes and subsequent short-term lapses in PrEP adherence and increases in sexual risk behaviors. We hypothesize that these mechanisms are heterogeneous and identifiable via intensive longitudinal data.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 MSM who engage in chemsex and are on PrEP for a 6-month Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) study. Participants will complete brief smartphone surveys 4 times daily (random + event-based following substance use/sex), reporting on substance use, sexual behavior, affect, and context. EMA data will be time-linked to electronic health records for PrEP pharmacy refills and quarterly STI/HIV testing results. We will use multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling to analyze temporal networks and dynamic structural equation modeling (DSEM) to test mediation pathways. A subsample (n=30) will complete periodic in-depth interviews to explore structural drivers and decision-making.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, mechanistic evidence of how chemsex contexts dynamically influence HIV-related health outcomes in real time. It will move beyond static associations to identify actionable, situational intervention points. The integrated mixed-methods data will inform the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) and contextually sensitive clinical counselling strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hart et al. has examined predictors of starting/stopping chemsex, this study uniquely applies EMA and temporal network analysis to unpack the real-time, within-person processes. It innovatively links momentary behavioral data directly to clinical biomarkers, a method not yet widely applied in chemsex research pre-2026.

proposals.title: A targeted behavioural intervention to reduce high-risk sexual behaviours and STI incidence among HIV-positive men who have sex with men engaging in chemsex: a pilot randomised controlled trial
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence indicates that HIV-positive men who have sex with men (MSM) engaging in chemsex constitute a high-risk subgroup with complex health needs, facing elevated risks for sexually transmitted infections (STIs), poor HIV management, and mental health comorbidities. Existing broad public health interventions are often insufficiently tailored to address the unique intersection of substance use, sexual risk, and HIV status in this population, creating a significant gap in targeted care.
proposals.hypothesis: A theory-based, digitally delivered behavioural intervention specifically tailored for HIV-positive MSM who engage in chemsex will be feasible, acceptable, and will lead to a greater reduction in self-reported high-risk sexual behaviours and biologically confirmed STI incidence over 12 months, compared to standard of care information provision.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pilot two-arm randomised controlled trial (RCT). Approximately 120 HIV-positive MSM reporting recent chemsex will be recruited via sexual health clinics and online networks. Participants will be randomised 1:1 to receive either a 12-week interactive text-message intervention (adapted from the safetxt model) with content addressing chemsex-related risk, HIV treatment adherence, and mental wellbeing, or a control arm of standard health information leaflets. Primary outcomes will be feasibility (recruitment, retention, intervention engagement) and acceptability. Secondary outcomes include changes in condomless sex with serodiscordant partners, chemsex frequency, and STI incidence at 6 and 12 months, analysed using intention-to-treat mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first RCT evidence on the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of a targeted intervention for this high-risk subgroup, directly addressing a gap identified in Hart's prior chemsex predictors research. It will yield a manualised, scalable intervention model that integrates HIV care with chemsex harm reduction, informing future definitive trials and clinical guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies like AURAH2 have identified predictors of chemsex, and safetxt demonstrated efficacy in a general youth population, this proposal novelly combines these strands to develop and test a dedicated intervention for the specific, high-risk intersection of HIV-positive status and chemsex, a focus not yet addressed by pre-2026 RCTs.

proposals.title: Long-term sexual health outcomes and associated risk behaviours following a PrEP adherence intervention: a five-year follow-up of the SHARE trial participants
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, while trials like SHARE demonstrate short-term efficacy of behavioural interventions for PrEP adherence and sexual health, the durability of these effects is unknown. Long-term follow-up is critical to understand if initial benefits are sustained, decay, or evolve, particularly in the context of dynamic sexual behaviours like chemsex and changing STI epidemiology. This addresses a key evidence gap for designing effective, long-lasting public health strategies for HIV prevention among MSM.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the positive effects of the SHARE trial intervention on PrEP adherence and reduced sexual risk behaviours will be sustained at five-year follow-up, but that this effect will be moderated by longitudinal engagement in chemsex. We further hypothesise that long-term STI incidence will be associated with distinct behavioural trajectories post-intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study re-contacting all original SHARE trial participants approximately five years post-baseline. Primary outcomes are biochemically verified PrEP adherence (via hair samples), self-reported sexual behaviours, and clinic-recorded STI diagnoses. We will use latent class growth analysis (LCGA) to model trajectories of chemsex engagement and PrEP use over time. Multivariable logistic and Poisson regression will assess associations between intervention arm, long-term behavioural trajectories, and clinical outcomes, adjusting for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence on the durability of a PrEP adherence intervention, informing the need for booster sessions or adapted long-term support. It will identify distinct behavioural trajectories (e.g., sustained low-risk, escalating chemsex) that predict long-term sexual health, enabling targeted interventions. The findings will directly influence guidelines on the long-term management of MSM on PrEP.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AURAH2) examines predictors of chemsex, this study uniquely integrates long-term trial follow-up with trajectory modelling to understand how an intervention interacts with evolving risk behaviours over a clinically meaningful timeframe, moving beyond static, cross-sectional associations.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model of Peer Influence on PrEP and Chemsex Trajectories among Men Who Have Sex with Men in England
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, identifies chemsex and STI risk as critical public health issues among MSM. While individual-level predictors are known, the role of dynamic social network processes in shaping both risk behaviors (like chemsex initiation/cessation) and protective intervention uptake (like PrEP) is poorly understood, limiting the design of effective, scalable interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Simulated social influence and network homophily mechanisms within an agent-based model will generate population-level patterns of PrEP uptake and chemsex engagement that match empirical longitudinal cohort data, and will identify network-based intervention points more efficient than individual-level targeting.
proposals.method: We will develop an open-source ABM using NetLogo or a Python library (Mesa). Agents will have attributes (e.g., PrEP use, chemsex status) and be connected in a dynamic network initialized with AURAH2 study data. Core algorithms will include: 1) a bounded confidence model for opinion diffusion on PrEP acceptability, 2) a peer exposure function for chemsex behavior adoption, and 3) a rewiring rule for network homophily. The model will be calibrated and validated against AURAH2's observed temporal trends.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel computational framework to test 'what-if' scenarios for network-informed interventions (e.g., seeding PrEP influencers) before costly real-world trials. It will move the field beyond identifying associated factors to formally modeling the causal processes of social influence on sexual health.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are used in epidemiology, their application to model the co-evolution of chemsex behaviors and PrEP adoption within MSM social networks is novel. This integrates Dr. Hart's expertise in behavioral trials and chemsex with computational social science methods to address a core gap in mechanism.

proposals.title: A randomised controlled trial of a dual-component behavioural intervention (Partner-Parent Reinforce) to reduce sexual risk and chemsex-related harms among adolescent men who have sex with men (AMSM)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, shows that school-based sexual health education often fails to reach adolescent MSM effectively, a group with elevated risks for STIs, HIV, and emerging chemsex behaviours. Structural factors, including lack of supportive disclosure to parents and peer/partner dynamics, are critical barriers. This study addresses the 'missing' engagement noted in tuberculosis research by targeting two key social ecosystems simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: A behavioural intervention that concurrently educates and supports both trusted sexual partners and parents of AMSM will be more effective in reinforcing school-based health messages and reducing sexual risk behaviours and chemsex initiation than standard school-based education alone.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-site, parallel-group randomised controlled trial. AMSM (aged 16-19) participants will be recruited via sexual health clinics and online platforms and randomly assigned to intervention or control (standard care). The intervention comprises: 1) A structured, dyadic counselling session for the participant and a nominated sexual partner, focusing on communication, PrEP adherence, and chemsex risk reduction, adapted from the safetxt framework. 2) A separate, tailored guidance session for a participating parent/guardian, delivered via telehealth. Primary outcomes are STI incidence and self-reported chemsex engagement at 12 months, analysed using intention-to-treat with logistic regression models, adjusting for baseline predictors as in the AURAH2 study.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first RCT evidence on the efficacy of a dual-ecosystem approach for AMSM sexual health. It will generate a manualised intervention model for engaging partners and parents, filling a critical gap between individual-level behavioural trials and structural interventions. Findings will directly inform integrated prevention strategies for HIV, STIs, and substance use in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While partner-based and parent-based interventions exist in isolation, their concurrent, integrated application to reinforce school messages for AMSM is novel. It uniquely bridges Dr. Hart's expertise in chemsex predictors, behavioural RCTs (safetxt), and structural intervention targeting.

proposals.title: Comparative effectiveness of drug use screening in HIV care versus non-HIV primary care settings for men who have sex with men: a prospective cohort study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, drug use, particularly chemsex, is a significant risk factor for poor health outcomes and onward HIV transmission among MSM. While HIV clinics are often targeted for screening, this may miss a substantial population of MSM not engaged in HIV care, including those on PrEP or not in care at all. Identifying the most effective setting is crucial for designing efficient public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that screening for drug use in non-HIV primary care settings (e.g., sexual health clinics, GP practices) will identify a larger and demographically distinct population of MSM who use drugs compared to screening exclusively in HIV specialist clinics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study recruiting MSM from three settings: HIV outpatient clinics, sexual health clinics, and primary care. Participants will complete a baseline behavioural survey, including the Alcohol, Smoking and Substance Involvement Screening Test (ASSIST), and be followed for 12 months. Using multivariable logistic regression, we will model the primary outcome (identification of moderate/high-risk drug use) by recruitment setting, adjusting for demographics, sexual behaviours, and HIV/PrEP status. We will also conduct a cost-effectiveness analysis of screening across settings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the yield and efficiency of drug screening across different healthcare contexts. It will inform clinical and public health guidelines on where to optimally deploy limited resources for substance use intervention. The findings will challenge the current clinic-centric model and support a more integrated, person-centred approach to health for MSM.
proposals.novelty: This directly tests the assumed primacy of HIV clinics for drug screening, a premise rarely questioned in pre-2026 literature. It leverages the researcher's expertise in trials and behavioural studies to compare settings, moving beyond simply describing drug use within a single clinical population.

proposals.title: Mental Health and Stigma as Primary Drivers of Sexual Risk in MSM who use Drugs: A Mediation Analysis of the AURAH2 Cohort
proposals.motivation: Research on chemsex and sexual health in men who have sex with men (MSM) often focuses on drug use as the primary behavioral risk factor. However, syndemic theory and pre-2026 evidence suggest that mental health issues (e.g., depression, anxiety) and minority stress (e.g., sexual stigma) may be more fundamental drivers of both drug use and high-risk sexual behaviors. Understanding this causal pathway is critical for developing more effective, holistic interventions that move beyond substance-focused harm reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between polydrug use and sexual risk behaviors (e.g., condomless sex, multiple partners) is significantly mediated by underlying mental health symptoms and experiences of sexual stigma, such that the direct effect of drug use is attenuated when these psychosocial factors are accounted for.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the AURAH2 prospective cohort study, which includes detailed longitudinal data on MSM in England. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a mediation model where mental health (PHQ-9, GAD-7 scores) and stigma (adapted scales for sexual minority stress) are specified as mediators between baseline polydrug use (a latent variable constructed from substance use frequency) and subsequent sexual risk outcomes. The analysis will control for known confounders (age, PrEP use, HIV status) and use bootstrapping (10,000 iterations) to test the significance of indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence to shift the conceptual framework from a drug-centric to a syndemic model of risk in MSM. It will identify specific, modifiable psychosocial targets (e.g., depression, internalized stigma) for future behavioral interventions, potentially informing integrated care models that combine mental health support with sexual health services.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established correlations, this analysis directly tests a causal mediation model to statistically disentangle whether drug use is a primary driver or a concurrent symptom of deeper psychosocial distress, inverting the typical analytical focus of chemsex research.

proposals.title: Beyond Condomless Sex: A Multi-Dimensional Risk Score for Adverse Sexual Health Outcomes Among MSM Using PrEP
proposals.motivation: In the era of highly effective HIV prevention (PrEP and U=U), the singular focus on condomless sex as the primary risk metric is outdated and clinically insufficient. This narrow focus fails to capture other significant adverse outcomes, such as bacterial STI acquisition, psychological distress, and substance-related harms, particularly within contexts like chemsex. A more holistic measure is needed to guide behavioural interventions and clinical care.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite risk score incorporating behavioural, psychological, and substance-use factors will predict a composite adverse outcome (incident STI, chemsex-related harm, or sexual distress) more accurately than the metric of condomless sex alone among MSM using PrEP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal data from the AURAH2 study, enriched with targeted follow-up surveys. Using logistic regression and machine learning (Random Forest), we will develop a predictive model. The composite outcome variable will be time to first adverse event (STI diagnosis, self-reported chemsex harm, or high score on the Sexual Distress Scale). Predictor variables will include chemsex frequency, polydrug use, mental health scores, partner number, and PrEP adherence, moving beyond condom use as the sole behavioural input.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, multi-dimensional tool for assessing sexual health risk, shifting the field towards a more nuanced understanding of 'risk' in the prevention era. It will directly inform the development of future behavioural interventions (like safetxt) by identifying modifiable risk factors beyond condom use for targeted messaging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work predicts condomless sex or STIs individually, this study explicitly challenges its primacy as an endpoint and innovatively combines biomedical, behavioural, and psychological outcomes into a single patient-centred risk metric relevant to contemporary MSM health.

proposals.title: A mixed-methods study of HIV care engagement, chemsex, and PrEP use among ethnically diverse and non-gay-identifying men who have sex with men living with HIV in England
proposals.motivation: Research on HIV-diagnosed MSM, particularly regarding behaviours like chemsex and PrEP use, has historically relied on samples of white, gay-identified men. This limits understanding of health risks and engagement with care among ethnically diverse and non-gay-identifying (e.g., bisexual, queer, or heterosexual-identifying) MSM, who may face unique structural and cultural barriers. Addressing this evidence gap is critical for developing equitable, targeted public health interventions to improve outcomes across the entire MSM population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ethnically diverse and non-gay-identifying MSM living with HIV will report distinct patterns of chemsex participation, PrEP use, and HIV care engagement compared to white, gay-identified MSM, and that these differences will be associated with specific structural and psychosocial factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study with a targeted recruitment strategy using community-based venues, online platforms, and sexual health clinics to enrol a diverse sample of 500 HIV-diagnosed MSM in England. Participants will complete a baseline and 12-month follow-up survey assessing chemsex behaviours, PrEP use, ART adherence, sexual health, and validated scales for stigma, mental health, and healthcare access. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of chemsex and suboptimal care engagement, and purposively sample 30-40 participants for in-depth interviews to explore structural and identity-related influences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, quantitative data on chemsex trajectories and PrEP use in a diverse sample of HIV-diagnosed MSM in the UK. It will generate novel evidence on how sexual identity and ethnicity intersect to shape health behaviours and care engagement, directly informing the development of more inclusive behavioural interventions and clinical communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies like AURAH2 have examined chemsex predictors, none have prospectively focused on recruiting a pre-specified, diverse sample of HIV-diagnosed MSM to explicitly compare experiences across ethnic and sexual identity subgroups, integrating quantitative behavioural data with qualitative exploration of structural drivers.

proposals.title: Mapping the Diffusion of Chemsex and Associated Sexual Health Behaviours in MSM Communities: A Social Network Analysis of the AURAH2 Cohort
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, associated with increased risk of HIV/STI transmission and poor mental health. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, has identified individual-level predictors but lacks an understanding of how these behaviours spread through social and sexual networks, limiting the design of effective, community-level interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption and cessation of chemsex, as well as associated protective behaviours like PrEP use, are significantly influenced by the structure and composition of an individual's social and sexual networks, with diffusion following identifiable pathways through key network positions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the prospective AURAH2 study data, constructing ego-centric networks from participant-reported data on sexual partners and social contacts. Using exponential random graph models (ERGMs) and stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) via the `statnet` and `RSiena` packages, we will model network formation and the co-evolution of network ties with individual attributes (chemsex engagement, PrEP use, sexual risk behaviours). This will identify influential network structures and the role of key actors (e.g., opinion leaders, bridges) in behavioural diffusion.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of how chemsex behaviours diffuse within UK MSM networks, moving beyond individual risk factors to a relational understanding. It will identify potential network-based intervention points, such as targeting individuals in brokerage positions for peer-led harm reduction messaging, thereby informing more effective public health strategies.
proposals.novelty: While social network analysis is established in sociology, its application to model the dynamic co-evolution of chemsex and sexual health behaviours in a UK MSM cohort is novel, integrating behavioural epidemiology with structural sociology to explain diffusion processes that individual-level models cannot capture.

proposals.title: Design and pilot implementation of a national sentinel surveillance system for drug use and sexual health among people living with HIV in England
proposals.motivation: The syndemic of HIV, chemsex (sexualised drug use), and STIs among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a critical public health challenge. Pre-2026, data on these intersecting risks are fragmented, often from localised or time-limited studies like AURAH2, limiting the ability to monitor trends, target interventions, and evaluate the impact of PrEP scale-up. A systematic, ongoing national surveillance mechanism is urgently needed to inform policy and service provision.
proposals.hypothesis: A standardised, multi-site sentinel surveillance system, embedded within routine HIV care, can feasibly generate timely, representative, and actionable data on the prevalence, trends, and health outcomes associated with chemsex and sexual risk behaviours in people living with HIV.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods implementation study. First, we will use consensus methods with key stakeholders (clinicians, public health agencies, community representatives) to define a core minimum dataset and reporting protocols. Subsequently, we will pilot the system in a purposive sample of 10-12 high-HIV-caseload clinics across England. The technical approach involves integrating a brief, anonymised behavioural questionnaire (covering drug use, sexual behaviours, and STI diagnoses) into routine clinical encounters, with data encrypted and transmitted to a centralised, secure database. We will employ time-series analysis to monitor trends and logistic regression to identify factors associated with adverse outcomes (e.g., STI reinfection).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first operational blueprint for a national real-time behavioural surveillance system specific to HIV-diagnosed populations. It will generate pilot data on the feasibility and utility of such a system, producing initial estimates of chemsex prevalence and its association with STI acquisition in the post-PrEP era, directly informing the development of targeted harm reduction interventions.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies like AURAH2 have provided snapshot data, this proposal moves from observational research to designing a permanent, scalable surveillance infrastructure. It innovatively leverages the existing HIV care system as a sentinel network to monitor a rapidly evolving behavioural risk landscape, a method not yet implemented nationally in this context.

proposals.title: Predicting Chemsex Engagement in MSM: A Machine Learning Analysis of Sociodemographic and Clinical Predictors from the AURAH2 Study
proposals.motivation: Chemsex among men who have sex with men (MSM) is a significant public health concern, associated with increased risk of HIV/STI transmission and mental health harms. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hart's work, has largely examined the consequences of chemsex. To design effective, targeted interventions, it is crucial to identify the key factors that predict who engages in chemsex, moving beyond descriptive associations to predictive modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, intersecting sociodemographic (e.g., age, ethnicity, deprivation), clinical (e.g., PrEP use, HIV status, mental health), and behavioural factors can be used to build a robust predictive model for chemsex engagement among MSM in England.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the AURAH2 prospective study data. Using chemsex status (starting/stopping/ongoing) as the primary outcome, we will train and compare multiple machine learning classifiers, including regularized logistic regression (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), to identify the most predictive feature set. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation, assessing accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC-ROC. Key predictors will be interpreted via feature importance metrics and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a validated predictive model for chemsex, identifying the most salient risk and protective factors. It will directly inform the development of more precise public health messaging and screening tools for clinicians serving MSM communities, shifting the field from risk association to risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified correlates of chemsex, this work inverts the analytical framework to explicitly model prediction, applying contemporary machine learning techniques for feature selection and interpretation in this specific behavioral domain, which remains underexplored pre-2026.

ideas.title: Traditional Harm Reduction and the Social Meaning of HIV-Negative Status Among Young Women Who Use Drugs
ideas.description: This study investigates contexts where young women who use drugs and have seroconverted employ traditional, non-clinical methods (e.g., peer-based monitoring, ritual, or heuristic rules) to manage their health and drug use, specifically to maintain an HIV-negative status. It explores whether the reliance on these traditional methods, as opposed to modern biomedical interventions like PrEP, is more empowering or effective for them in navigating risk, and how the potential disruption of a stable 'HIV-negative' identity post-seroconversion alters these traditional practices and associated drug use patterns. The research uniquely questions the assumption that modern methods are inherently superior by centering the lived experience of identity stability and its intersection with culturally embedded harm reduction strategies.

ideas.title: Systemic Barriers to STI Prevention in Chemsex Contexts: Beyond Individual Risk
ideas.description: This research investigates how health system structures—including clinic hours, testing protocols, provider attitudes, and linkage-to-care pathways—actively create barriers to effective STI prevention, testing, and treatment for individuals engaged in chemsex. It moves beyond correlating drug use with infection rates to examine how systemic design (e.g., lack of integrated sexual health and substance use services, judgmental clinical environments, and fragmented care) discourages engagement and perpetuates transmission of bacterial STIs. The study employs a mixed-methods approach, combining health services data with qualitative accounts from service users and providers, to identify specific structural disincentives. The goal is to generate evidence for system-level interventions that reduce STI incidence by making prevention and care accessible and appropriate for chemsex-practicing communities.

ideas.title: Mechanistic and Contextual Longitudinal Study of Chemsex, PrEP Trajectories, and Sexual Health Outcomes
ideas.description: This study prospectively follows multiple cohorts of GBMSM, differentiating by PrEP status and chemsex engagement at baseline, to map evolving trajectories. It integrates annual quantitative surveys with embedded qualitative interviews and social network mapping at key intervals to uncover the psychological, social, and intentional drivers behind changes in both chemsex practices and PrEP use. The analysis employs time-lagged models and carefully controls for confounding variables to better discern causal pathways and the mechanisms through which chemsex influences, or is influenced by, PrEP adherence and sexual health outcomes over time.

ideas.title: MECH: Mechanisms of Chemsex Contexts on HIV Health Outcomes in MSM
ideas.description: This study implements a longitudinal, mixed-methods Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) design to investigate the real-time contextual mechanisms linking polydrug use (chemsex) to HIV clinical and behavioral outcomes among MSM. Participants complete brief smartphone surveys multiple times daily for several months, reporting on substance use, sexual activity, affect, and social context, which is linked to electronic health records for biomarker data. Concurrent periodic in-depth interviews explore meaning, decision-making, and structural drivers. The integrated data enables analysis of temporal pathways and specific situational triggers to inform precisely timed, context-aware behavioral and biomedical interventions.

ideas.title: Leveraging Negative Association as a Behavioral Catalyst
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying an analogy from behavioral economics and public health marketing. The analogous problem is motivating avoidance of a high-risk behavior (like smoking) by leveraging negative associations (graphic health warnings). The solution pairs the vivid threat with a clear, actionable solution. Adapted to condom use, the research would explore designing and testing interventions that deliberately harness the condom's disease-prevention association. The intervention would target situations individuals already perceive as high-risk, using messaging that explicitly connects the condom to mitigating that specific, salient risk. This approach aims to transform the 'disease association' from a broad social stigma into a context-specific facilitator, positioning the condom not as a symbol of promiscuity but as the intelligent, responsible tool for a defined risk scenario, thereby increasing its likelihood of use in those precise moments.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of chemsex and PrEP use using a behavioral cascade model adapted from climate change adaptation research
ideas.description: This research adapts a longitudinal behavioral cascade model from climate change adaptation science to study the relationship between chemsex drug use and PrEP adoption. The analogous problem—understanding how communities adopt new technologies in response to evolving environmental stressors—is solved by modeling the interaction between stressor perception, solution access, and social norms over time. The adapted idea is to conduct a prospective cohort study that tracks how changes in the perception of HIV risk during chemsex, access to PrEP services, and evolving social norms within participants' networks interact in a cascading fashion to predict the timing, level, and sustainability of PrEP uptake and adherence over a multi-year period.

ideas.title: Dynamic Agent-Based Modeling of Chemsex Trajectories and HIV Seroconversion
ideas.description: This research synthesizes agent-based modeling with a focus on HIV status transition to create a novel simulation of chemsex communities. The model populates agents with realistic, data-informed trajectories, where an agent's drug use patterns, sexual network behavior, and engagement with harm reduction services can evolve upon seroconversion. This allows for testing how interventions (e.g., PrEP, peer support, drug checking) perform not just in a static population, but in one where individuals' risks and motivations dynamically change with HIV status. The core innovation is predicting how the population-level impact of interventions is amplified or dampened by these post-seroconversion behavioral feedback loops within sexual networks.

ideas.description: This study applies cognitive interviewing techniques, a qualitative survey methodology, to investigate how men who have sex with men (MSM) in smaller cities and rural areas understand and interpret sexual health terms, service information, and public health messaging. By moving the methodological focus from young women (Idea B) to MSM in non-metropolitan settings (Idea A), it directly addresses a gap in how health literacy is shaped by localized social environments and potentially limited community resources. The synergy allows for the identification of not just *what* is misunderstood, but *why* certain terms or concepts are interpreted differently outside of major urban hubs like London, linking comprehension to local context. This approach generates evidence to tailor health communications and service descriptions for improved accessibility and effectiveness in underserved MSM populations.

ideas.title: Adapting Respondent-Driven Sampling from Hard-to-Reach Epidemiology to Assess Polydrug Use in Non-Clinic MSM
ideas.description: This research adapts Respondent-Driven Sampling (RDS), a solution from the epidemiology of hard-to-reach populations like people who inject drugs, to assess polydrug and chemsex prevalence in MSM not attending sexual health clinics. The analogous solution uses peer-referral chains with incentives and statistical weighting to mitigate sampling bias. The adapted idea involves recruiting initial 'seed' participants from non-clinic venues and using a structured RDS protocol to build a sample, collecting survey data on drug use behaviors, and applying RDS analytical techniques to generate population-representative prevalence estimates for this otherwise hidden subgroup.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Study Behavioral Stability Post-Seroconversion
ideas.description: This idea adapts a framework from Community Ecology to challenge the assumption that HIV-negative status is a stable behavioral category. In ecology, researchers distinguish between a species' fundamental and realized niche, often testing niche conservatism by observing behavior after a competitor's removal. Analogously, we can study individuals who seroconvert to understand if their drug use patterns (their 'realized behavioral niche') change with HIV status, or if they remain stable, suggesting that other social, structural, or psychological factors define the 'fundamental' behavioral niche. This approach reframes seroconversion as a natural experiment removing a perceived constraint (fear of HIV infection), testing the plasticity versus stability of risk behaviors.

ideas.title: Biologically-validated causal modeling of chemsex risk pathways
ideas.description: This study integrates biological sampling (e.g., toxicology, STI testing) with detailed psychosocial surveys in a chemsex cohort to create a validated dataset. By anchoring self-reported drug use and sexual behavior to objective measures, it establishes a high-fidelity foundation for causal inference modeling. The core analysis then tests competing models: one where specific drugs directly elevate risk versus models where mental health or social isolation are the primary drivers, with drug use acting as a mediator or behavioral outcome. This approach moves beyond correlation to identify the most potent, biologically-grounded intervention targets for harm reduction.

ideas.title: Beyond the Substance: Testing the 'Vector' vs. 'Environment' Model of Chemsex Harm
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that specific chemsex drugs are the primary risk factor by drawing an analogy from infectious disease epidemiology. In that field, the solution to understanding disease spread involved moving beyond the pathogen to model the interaction of host susceptibility and environmental context. Adapting this, the study would test whether mental health (host factor) and social isolation (environmental factor) are stronger predictors of chemsex-related harm than the specific drugs used. The research would employ multivariate regression or structural equation modeling on cohort data to compare the predictive power of pharmacological, psychological, and sociological variables, potentially reframing intervention strategies from substance-focused to person- and context-focused.

ideas.title: Iterative Bio-Behavioral Calibration: Using Biomarker Validation to Refine and Ground Culturally-Informed Survey Measures
ideas.description: This research program develops and validates culturally grounded survey measures for sensitive behaviors like drug use and sexual activity through an iterative, multi-method process. First, qualitative themes from the target population inform the initial draft of survey items. These items are then administered in a study that concurrently collects biological samples (e.g., urine drug screens, STI tests) to objectively measure the reported behaviors. The biomarker data is used not merely to assess overall accuracy, but to perform item-level psychometric analysis, identifying which qualitatively-derived phrasings, contexts, or response scales best predict objective biological outcomes. The result is a calibrated set of survey measures whose validity is empirically demonstrated, enabling more accurate and representative large-scale surveillance of hard-to-measure behaviors.

ideas.title: Agent-based modeling of chemsex interventions using epidemiological network adaptation from wildlife conservation
ideas.description: This research adapts agent-based modeling (ABM) solutions from wildlife conservation and population ecology to simulate chemsex harm reduction interventions. In the analogous field, ABMs successfully predict the population-level impact of interventions (e.g., targeted vaccination, habitat change) on disease spread in animal populations with complex social structures. The adapted idea constructs an ABM where individuals engaged in chemsex are modeled as agents with attributes (sexual risk behaviors, substance use patterns, network position) and interaction rules within a dynamic sexual/social network. Interventions like peer navigation, pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) targeting, and drug checking are simulated as events that modify agent rules. The model, calibrated with empirical data, will simulate different intervention rollouts to forecast their comparative effectiveness in reducing population-level HIV/STI incidence and acute harm, thereby informing public health strategy and resource allocation for chemsex harm reduction.

ideas.title: Network Dynamics of Chemsex and PrEP Adoption: A Longitudinal Social Influence Study
ideas.description: This study prospectively tracks a cohort of individuals within their interconnected social and sexual networks to analyze how engagement in chemsex and adoption of PrEP co-evolve and diffuse over time. It applies social network analysis to map how norms, information, and behaviors related to substance use and PrEP are transmitted through peer and partner ties. The longitudinal design allows the research to distinguish between social influence (where a peer's behavior affects one's own) and social selection (where individuals form ties with similar others), specifically for chemsex and PrEP use. This reveals the critical network pathways and influential actors that accelerate or inhibit PrEP uptake within communities where chemsex is present, informing network-tailored intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Shared Determinants and Resilience: A Causal Framework for Co-occurring Health Risks
ideas.description: This research models the shared upstream social and structural determinants (e.g., childhood adversity, economic instability, discrimination) that jointly influence patterns of both substance use and sexual risk behavior, rather than inferring one from the other. It employs a causal inference approach with directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to disentangle confounding from true causal pathways, integrating data on protective factors like social support and service access. The output is a stratified risk-resilience landscape that identifies specific contextual factors where interventions on shared determinants could most effectively reduce multiple co-occurring risks, moving beyond stigmatizing behavioral correlations.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Models to Map Contraceptive Knowledge Diffusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the classic epidemiological compartmental modeling approach (e.g., SIR models) to the sociological problem of contraceptive knowledge diffusion. In the analogous solution, disease spread is modeled through populations via contact rates and transmission probabilities. Adapted to this context, young women in a network are categorized into compartments representing their knowledge/behavior state (e.g., Unaware, Knowledgeable, Adopter). Using social network analysis data on peer and familial ties, the model defines 'transmission' rates for knowledge between connected individuals, parameterized by relationship type, frequency of contact, and social homophily. This computational model allows researchers to simulate diffusion pathways, identify critical network nodes for accelerating knowledge spread, and predict the impact of network-based intervention campaigns, providing a quantitative tool for public health strategy.

ideas.title: Challenge that modern methods are always preferable
ideas.description: Investigate contexts where traditional methods are more empowering or effective for young women than assumed.
ideas.sourceTitle: Limits to modern contraceptive use among young women in developing countries: a systematic review of qualitative research

ideas.title: Solve inverse problem: infer behavior from incidence
ideas.description: Use the model inversely to estimate unobserved trends in risk behavior from incidence data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Increased HIV Incidence in Men Who Have Sex with Men Despite High Levels of ART-Induced Viral Suppression: Analysis of an Extensively Documented Epidemic

ideas.title: Applying the COM-B Model to Chemsex: A Qualitative Analysis of Capability, Opportunity, and Motivation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by applying an analogical solution from behavioral science. The analogous problem is understanding the multifaceted drivers of other complex health behaviors, which is often addressed using the COM-B (Capability, Opportunity, Motivation -> Behavior) model. This framework provides a systematic structure for qualitative analysis. The adapted idea is to employ the COM-B model to qualitatively explore the reasons for chemsex drug use. Researchers would collect data through in-depth interviews with individuals who engage in chemsex and then code the findings into the model's components: physical and psychological capability, social and physical opportunity, and reflective and automatic motivation. This structured approach will move beyond a simple list of motivations to generate a nuanced, theoretically informed map of the behavior's drivers. The output will directly inform the selection of targeted intervention functions (e.g., training, environmental design, persuasion) to develop more effective and tailored public health interventions aimed at harm reduction and behavior change.

ideas.title: Use digital ethnography for online sexual spaces
ideas.description: Apply ethnographic methods to study culture and interaction within specific online chatrooms or dating platforms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Reflecting on the experience of interviewing online: perspectives from the Internet and HIV study in London

ideas.title: Applying Syndemic Theory and Network Analysis to Model Chemsex as a Driver of Multi-Pathogen STI Clusters
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to syndemic approaches in respiratory disease epidemiology. The analogous solution involves using syndemic theory and contact network analysis to model how host behaviors and environmental settings drive clusters of co-circulating pathogens. Adapted to the chemsex context, the research idea is to apply syndemic theory and network analysis to model chemsex as a driver of multi-pathogen STI clusters. The study would investigate associations between chemsex drug use and the incidence of a suite of other STIs (e.g., syphilis, gonorrhea, LGV, HSV, HCV, mpox) by mapping social-sexual networks and employing multiplex testing. The goal is to determine if chemsex environments create syndemic-like conditions that amplify the concurrent transmission of multiple STIs, moving beyond a single-pathogen or HIV-centric focus to understand the broader infectious disease burden.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Intervention to Counteract Contraceptive Fear in Real-Time
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a smartphone-based ecological momentary intervention (EMI) that fuses real-time monitoring with targeted psychoeducation. The system uses ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to collect data on mood, situational triggers, and sexual health behaviors, including contraceptive use avoidance. When patterns indicative of fear-driven decision-making (e.g., avoiding contraception due to infertility myths prior to a sexual encounter) are detected, the system delivers tailored, evidence-based micro-interventions to dispel specific myths in the moment. This allows for the evaluation of how real-time, contextual correction of misinformation impacts contraceptive adherence and reduces perceived risk, creating a closed-loop system for behavioral intervention.

ideas.title: Challenge that knowledge is irrelevant to testing uptake

ideas.title: Cognitive Interviewing to Map Evolving Contraceptive Understanding and Decision-Making in the Transition to Long-Term Relationships (Ages 18-30)
ideas.description: This study employs cognitive interviewing techniques to investigate how women aged 18-30 understand and process contraceptive and sexual health information, specifically focusing on how this understanding shifts as they transition from shorter-term to longer-term committed relationships. It moves beyond testing static comprehension of terms to dynamically explore how relationship stage, age, and life goals interact with cognitive schemas around risk, responsibility, and method suitability. The synergy reveals not just barriers to understanding, but how relational context actively shapes the interpretation and application of health information during a pivotal developmental window.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Inferring Substance Use from Behavioral Phenotypes via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the inverse problem in ecology—inferring species presence from environmental conditions—to the original idea of modeling drug use from risk behavior. In ecology, niche models (e.g., MaxEnt) predict where a species might be found based on known occurrences and habitat variables. Here, we analogously model the 'behavioral niche' of a drug. The research will use data on sexual risk behaviors (partner numbers, condom use, etc.), combined with other contextual factors, as the predictive 'environment.' A machine learning model will be trained on populations with confirmed drug use to learn the behavioral signature or niche associated with specific substances. The validated model can then be applied to broader behavioral survey data to probabilistically infer the prevalence of specific drug use in populations where direct measurement is difficult, enabling targeted interventions based on behavioral phenotypes rather than self-reported substance use.

ideas.title: Develop interventions targeting DES in MSM
ideas.description: Create and test interventions to reduce disordered eating risks based on identified factors like CSA and depression.
ideas.sourceTitle: Web-Based Behavioral Surveillance Among Men Who Have Sex With Men: A Comparison of Online and Offline Samples in London, UK

ideas.title: Dyadic Observational Study of Contraceptive Negotiation and Influence in Young Couples
ideas.description: This study investigates the real-time dynamics of contraceptive decision-making within young heterosexual couples using a multi-method, longitudinal design. Researchers will recruit established couples and employ a combination of periodic in-depth interviews, audio-diary methods, and structured observations of joint tasks related to contraceptive choices. The analysis will focus on interactional sequences, communication patterns, and the mobilization of social and material resources by both partners to map how influence and constraint are relationally produced and contested. This approach shifts the focus from static male perspectives to the observable, interactive processes that constitute control and negotiation within partnerships.

ideas.title: Cognitive Pretesting and Refinement of a Discrete Choice Experiment for Contraceptive Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research synthesizes cognitive interviewing and discrete choice experiments (DCEs) into a sequential, participant-centered design process. First, cognitive interviews with young women will probe their understanding and mental models of key contraceptive attributes (e.g., 'privacy,' 'effectiveness,' specific 'side effects') to identify misinterpretations and inform the authentic definition of attribute levels for the DCE. The refined DCE then quantifies the trade-offs between these validated attributes. The synergy lies in using qualitative insights to construct a more ecologically valid and comprehensible quantitative choice model, ultimately yielding preference weights that more accurately reflect decision-making based on correctly understood options.

ideas.title: Apply discrete choice experiments from marketing

ideas.title: Biologically-validated network analysis of chemsex diffusion and risk
ideas.description: This research fuses social network analysis with biological sampling to map and validate the diffusion of chemsex behaviors in MSM communities. By collecting biological samples (e.g., hair for drug metabolites, STI testing) alongside self-reported network and behavior data, the study can objectively validate both individual behaviors and the connections within the network. This allows for the construction of a 'ground-truth' network map that distinguishes between reputational links and actual risk-sharing pathways. The resulting model can identify key influencers in risk propagation and test the accuracy of peer-reported behavior, offering unprecedented precision for targeted public health interventions.

ideas.title: Dual-System Mapping of Chemsex: A Behavioral and Neurochemical Parallel Study
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Systems Ecology, where the parallel study of a keystone species' behavior and its environmental chemistry reveals ecosystem dynamics, this research adapts that solution. It proposes a dual-system, longitudinal study of HIV-positive MSM engaged in chemsex. One arm meticulously maps the external behavioral system: sexual networks, polydrug use sequences, and contexts. The parallel arm maps the internal physiological system through regular biomarker assays for stress (cortisol), inflammation (CRP, IL-6), and neurotransmitter activity (e.g., dopamine metabolites). By analyzing these two 'systems' in parallel, the study aims to identify how specific drug-behavior combinations trigger physiological states that, in turn, reinforce or alter future risk behavior, moving beyond correlation to model the feedback mechanisms within the chemsex ecosystem.

ideas.title: Co-designed narrative interventions to transform cultural models of fertility and contraceptive safety
ideas.description: This research employs a community-based participatory approach to address fears linking contraceptives to infertility. It begins with deep ethnographic engagement to understand the cultural narratives and social networks that sustain these fears, moving beyond a deficit model of misinformation. In partnership with community members, the project co-designs and tests narrative-based communication strategies delivered by trusted insiders, which reframe contraceptive safety within locally meaningful concepts of health, family, and bodily integrity. The final outcome is a culturally grounded intervention protocol that builds community trust and transforms perceptions, rather than just dispensing corrective facts.

ideas.title: Using Vaccine Hesitancy Communication Models to Counter Contraceptive Fertility Myths
ideas.description: This research idea adapts proven communication strategies from the public health challenge of vaccine hesitancy to address myths linking hormonal contraceptives to permanent infertility. The analogous solution involves using trusted local messengers and narrative, dialogue-based formats to overcome deeply held fears, which is more effective than top-down information dissemination. The adapted idea is to design and test a community-based education program where trained community health advocates facilitate small-group dialogues, integrating scientific facts with compelling personal testimonies from trusted individuals within the community who have used hormonal contraception and later conceived, thereby directly dispelling myths through relatable conversation and rebuilt trust.

ideas.title: Cross-Cultural Real-Time Contextual Analysis of Behavioral Risk
ideas.description: This study employs smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to collect real-time data on substance use, sexual encounters, and immediate contextual factors across diverse regions, including South Asia and Latin America. Concurrently, it integrates iterative qualitative interviews grounded in the EMA data to explore the subjective meaning and perceived barriers behind the recorded behaviors. This mixed-methods, longitudinal design allows for the analysis of how cultural norms and environmental contexts dynamically influence risk in the moment. The synergy creates a powerful framework to distinguish between universal mechanistic triggers and culturally embedded pathways, generating insights for context-sensitive, real-time interventions.

ideas.title: Event-Level Data Integration for National Chemsex Surveillance
ideas.description: This research develops and implements a novel surveillance framework that embeds a qualitative 'deep dive' component within a national quantitative system. Periodically, a subset of participants from the national surveillance who report chemsex are recruited for detailed event-level interviews, providing rich data on contexts, motivations, and combinations. These narrative insights are then thematically analyzed and used to create or refine standardized survey items, ensuring national metrics remain relevant and capture evolving practices. The result is a responsive, evidence-driven surveillance system that can rapidly identify new public health threats and inform targeted interventions for MSM.

ideas.title: Adapt Network Contagion Models from Epidemiology to Simulate Method Adoption
ideas.description: This idea adapts agent-based modeling solutions from epidemiology, specifically network contagion models like SIR, to the problem of understanding the adoption of traditional versus modern methods. In epidemiology, these models successfully show how individual-level factors (contact patterns, immunity) produce population-level disease dynamics. The adapted research idea transposes this framework: agents represent individuals or households, their 'states' correspond to stages of method adoption, and 'transmission' depends on social influence through networks, perceived benefits/barriers, and access to resources. By simulating these interactions, the model can identify tipping points for widespread adoption and test the effect of interventions (e.g., targeting key network influencers or reducing specific barriers) on the population-level pattern of method use, providing a mechanistic understanding of diffusion beyond aggregate statistical analysis.

ideas.title: Online interviews are inherently less valid

ideas.title: Adaptive Sentinel Network for Real-Time Chemsex and Polydrug Use Surveillance
ideas.description: This system establishes a national network of sentinel sexual health clinics and community-based organizations in key urban areas to monitor chemsex and polydrug use among MSM. It combines a lightweight core of standardized, recurring behavioral metrics with a rapid-cycle, localized assessment protocol at each sentinel site to identify new substances, combinations, and risk contexts. Data from these local assessments are aggregated and analyzed by a central coordination unit, which uses this intelligence to iteratively refine and update the core surveillance metrics and risk alerts. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive surveillance system that provides both national trend data and timely, locally relevant intelligence for public health action.

ideas.title: Apply 'Think-Aloud Protocol' from Usability Engineering to Test Contraceptive Information Comprehension
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'Think-Aloud Protocol' from Usability Engineering in Human-Computer Interaction to the context of contraceptive health communication. In the analogous field, users verbalize their thoughts while navigating an interface to reveal usability flaws. The adapted solution would involve recruiting young women and asking them to read standard contraceptive information materials (e.g., package inserts, clinic pamphlets, educational websites) while continuously verbalizing their understanding, questions, and interpretations of key terms and instructions. This method would move beyond simple question-answer interviews to capture the real-time cognitive processing of health information, identifying not just what is misunderstood but the specific reasoning leading to that misunderstanding. The findings would provide a direct, evidence-based blueprint for rewriting and redesigning patient-facing contraceptive information to align with the target audience's cognitive frameworks and literacy levels.
